actions.thought: Queen-Three offsuit is a very weak hand. Even though we are on the button in a 3-handed game, this hand falls outside of standard opening ranges due to its poor playability, lack of connectivity, and high chance of being dominated by better Queens or simply stronger hands. Folding is the standard and optimal play here.

QJo is a strong hand in a blind vs. blind situation. Since the button folded, I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and exert pressure on the Big Blind, rather than just completing and playing a limped pot out of position.

Defending the big blind with A-4 suited is a standard and profitable play. I have favorable pot odds to call the 200 chip raise, and being in position post-flop offers good equity realization. Playing passively to see a flop is the disciplined approach in the first hand of the tournament.

The flop of 5s 3s 8h is relatively low and doesn't connect with my QJo. Since I am out of position and have no real equity (no pair, no draw), a continuation bet is risky on a board that contains a flush draw and might have hit the Big Blind's calling range (small pairs or connectors). I will check to see how the opponent reacts and potentially take a free card or fold if facing significant aggression.

I have the nut flush draw on a board of 5s-3s-8h. With my opponent checking to me, this is an ideal spot to take the lead and build the pot. My equity is very high, and betting 300 into the 600-chip pot puts pressure on my opponent while allowing me to realize my equity effectively.

I have Queen-Jack offsuit and the flop of 5s 3s 8h does not connect with my hand at all. I have no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. My opponent, the Big Blind, has bet 300 into a 600 pot after I checked. Given that I am out of position and have very little equity to improve against a range that includes small pairs and flush draws, folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips.
I have a premium pocket pair (TT) on the button. In a 3-handed game, this is an extremely strong hand. I will raise to 3x the big blind to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Holding 9-4 suited in the small blind against a button open is a marginal situation. Playing out of position with a weak suited connector makes it difficult to realize equity post-flop, and calling commits chips into a potentially difficult situation against a wider button range. Folding is the disciplined play early in the tournament to preserve stack size and avoid awkward post-flop spots.

Facing a 3bb raise from the Button, I have 6s 4c in the Big Blind. This hand is a weak offsuit one-gapper and plays terribly out of position. The best action is to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Holding 5-7 offsuit on the button is a weak hand. With 147 hands left to play, it is optimal to preserve my stack rather than engage in speculative play with poor cards. Folding is the disciplined choice to wait for a better opportunity.

Jack-Four offsuit is a weak hand. Playing it out of position from the Small Blind against the Big Blind is generally unprofitable. The best move is to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Ace-Queen suited is a premium starting hand, especially in a 3-handed game. Being on the button gives me a positional advantage post-flop. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard play to take the initiative, build the pot, and possibly win the blinds uncontested.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened for 3x (300). While A7o is a marginal hand to play out of position, it is strong enough to defend against a standard Button opening range. I will call the 250 to see a flop, keeping the pot size controlled while maintaining the opportunity to hit an Ace or a favorable board, though I must be cautious playing post-flop without initiative.

With 73 suited in the big blind, I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds to call (200 to win 700). Although this is a weak hand, the price is correct to defend the big blind in a multi-way pot, especially since I am closing the action.

I have Ace-high on a very dry board of 6-2-3. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and the Big Blind. There is no reason to lead out here as my hand is marginal and I want to see how the action develops. Checking allows me to evaluate Pro's continuation bet or Flashlight's action. Ace-high has decent showdown value on this board if it stays cheap.

I have bottom pair (a pair of threes) on a low, uncoordinated board. In a three-way pot, this hand has very marginal showdown value and is vulnerable to overcards. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or cause me to fold out bluffs I might want to keep in. Checking is the most strategic option to control the pot size, maintain my position as the last to act, and see a turn card cheaply.

Both opponents checked to me on a relatively dry, low board. While a continuation bet could fold out some missed hands, doing so into two opponents with only ace-high and backdoor draws carries some risk, as low boards can easily connect with the big blind's defending range. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card, realize my equity, and exercise pot control without risking chips when I have limited immediate value.

The turn brings the Jack of Clubs, an overcard to the flop. Given that the flop was checked around, it's unlikely anyone has a powerhouse hand, though a Jack could have improved the Button's range. I still only have Ace-high with no draw. There is no value in betting here, as I'm likely only called by better hands. I will check again to see how the other players react and try to get to a cheap showdown or potentially fold if there is significant betting action.

I hold a pair of threes (3h 7h) on a board of 6s 2h 3c Jc. This is a very marginal hand with minimal showdown value. With two opponents remaining, betting would likely only be called by better hands, and I have no significant draw to justify a semi-bluff. Checking is the optimal strategic decision to keep the pot controlled, minimize risk, and potentially see the river card for free.

With both opponents checking again on the turn, my Ace-Queen high still maintains some showdown value, but it lacks the strength to bet for value. The Jack of clubs introduces flush draws and straight possibilities, and betting here might only get called by better hands (like a Jack, a sticky pocket pair, or a strong draw) while forcing worse hands to fold. Taking another free card allows me to realize my equity by potentially hitting an Ace or Queen on the river without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

The hand has been checked all the way to the river on a relatively dry board. My Ace-high (A7o) has decent showdown value in a pot where no one has shown any aggression since the pre-flop raise. Betting wouldn't achieve much as better hands are unlikely to fold and worse hands are unlikely to call. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown, though I may have to fold if there is a significant bet from one of the other players.

I have a pair of threes on a 6-2-3-J-4 board. This is a very weak hand, and while it might beat some busted high-card hands, there is a high probability that either opponent holds a pair of fours, sixes, or higher that they were cautious with. Given that the hand has been checked down through all streets, it is likely that no one has a strong holding. Betting is risky because it would only be called by hands that beat me or cause opponents to fold hands I currently tie or beat. Checking behind is the optimal play to reach a showdown cheaply.

Both opponents have shown extreme passivity by checking all the way to the river. While my Ace-Queen high doesn't beat any pair, it beats missed draws and weaker Ace-highs that could be in their ranges. Betting here is risky because we would likely only be called by hands that beat us (like a small pair or a trapped Jack), and we don't need to turn our hand into a bluff when we have decent showdown value. Checking allows us to see the showdown for free.

I am on the button with 9s 4s in a 3-handed game. While the button allows for a wide opening range, 94s is generally too weak to open, even in a short-handed environment. My previous actions have shown some aggression, and I don't want to over-leverage a weak hand against the SB and BB who have healthy stacks. Folding here is the most disciplined play to maintain a solid table image and wait for better playability.

I am in the small blind with a weak starting hand (5s, Tc) after the button folded. With the pot already at 150 and only 50 needed to call, I have excellent pot odds to complete the blind and see a flop heads-up against the big blind. Raising here would likely be overplaying a marginal hand, so completing is the most efficient strategic play.

Opponent (Flashlight) limped from the Small Blind. I am in the Big Blind with Q5s. This is a mediocre hand but has some playability post-flop being suited. Raising could fold out some weaker hands or get called by better ones, bloating the pot unnecessarily. The most sensible option is to check, realize equity for free, and play the flop in position.

I am in the small blind with 5-high and a gutshot straight draw (need a 2 or a 6 for a straight) on a board of 3-4-J. The pot is small (200), and with a marginal hand that has little showdown value, checking is the most prudent play to see the next card or evaluate the opponent's reaction if they bet. Aggressive action with this weak range would likely be inefficient.

I have Queen high with a backdoor flush draw and some backdoor straight possibilities (any 2, 5, 6, or heart). Flashlight checked the flop after limping pre-flop, which often indicates weakness. However, I have very little immediate equity, and checking to see a free turn is a safe, standard play in a limped pot. I can realize my equity for free and re-evaluate on the turn if my draws materialize or if I want to attempt a delayed bluff.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a weak kicker on a 3-4-J-T board. Betting out of position with a marginal holding like this risks inflating the pot unnecessarily and potentially folding out hands I currently beat that might bluff on the river. Checking allows me to keep the pot size controlled and see a cheap river card, preserving my stack while remaining in a position to evaluate any bet from the opponent on the final street.

Opponent checked again on the turn. I hold Queen-high with no direct draws, as my backdoor flush draw missed and the board is somewhat coordinated. While a bet might fold out Ace-high or small pocket pairs, checking is lower risk, allows me to realize any remaining equity, and see the river for free. I can still evaluate whether to bluff on the river if the opponent checks a third time.

I hold a pair of Tens on a board of 3-4-J-T-9. Given that both players checked through the flop and turn, the action has remained passive. My hand possesses marginal showdown value, beating only absolute air or missed draws, while losing to any Jack, Nine, or better pair. Betting here would likely only be called by superior hands, turning my hand into an unnecessary bluff. Checking allows me to reach showdown affordably and maintain the pot size, which is the most disciplined approach given the board state.

Flashlight has checked every single street, showing extreme weakness and likely holding a missed draw, Ace-high, or a very weak pair. My Queen-high has very little showdown value and will likely lose to Ace-high or King-high. By making a well-sized river bluff of 150, I can apply pressure and fold out those better high-card hands or bottom pairs to steal the pot.

I am in the small blind with a pair of Tens and a weak kicker on a board of 3d-4h-Jc-Td-9c. The opponent, Pro, raised on the river after the hand was checked down through the flop and turn. This raise polarizes their range to either strong value hands or bluffs. Given my weak kicker, I am likely losing to any Jack, Nine, or a Ten with a better kicker, and splitting with any other Ten. Calling 150 into a pot of 350 is not mathematically sound with such low showdown value, as I am essentially a bluff-catcher that rarely wins. Folding is the most disciplined and efficient play to preserve my stack.
6-3 offsuit is a very weak, unplayable hand. It lacks connectivity and high-card strength. Folding is the standard, profitable decision to conserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 94o, an extremely weak offsuit hand. The Button has folded, and I am heads-up against the Big Blind. Even though it's only 50 chips to call, playing this trash hand out of position is unprofitable. The most sensible and +EV play here is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.
7s 3s is too weak of a hand to play from the Button in a 3-handed game, even though it's suited. The gap is large and the cards are low. A fold is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with K3o. The Button has folded, making this a blind vs blind situation. King-high is relatively strong heads-up, but the 3 kicker is weak. To put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the pot pre-flop, I will raise to 3x the big blind. This allows me to take the initiative if the hand continues.

I hold Ace-Eight offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a strong enough hand to defend against a Small Blind raise. The pot odds are favorable (2:1), and having position post-flop gives me a significant strategic advantage. Calling allows me to control the pot size and see the flop affordably.

The flop of 7d-5s-4d is quite coordinated and generally favors the Big Blind's defending range more than my Small Blind opening range. With only King-high and no direct draw, I do not have a strong enough hand to value bet, and a bluff might be ineffective against a range that contains many pairs and straight draws. I will check to see how the opponent reacts, hoping for a free turn card or to fold if they show significant strength.

The flop is highly coordinated with straight and flush possibilities, which interacts well with the Small Blind's raising range. My Ace-high is currently behind any pair, and betting into this board structure is a high-variance play with little immediate fold equity. By checking, I maintain pot control, get a free card, and avoid inflating a pot where I am currently weak. Checking is the most strategic play to realize equity or fold if the opponent shows strength on the turn.

The King on the turn is an excellent card for my range as the pre-flop aggressor, and it specifically gives me top pair. After both players checked the flop, I am likely ahead here. I will bet 300 (half the pot) to get value from weaker hands like middle pairs or draws (flush and straight draws) and to protect my equity against those draws.

I am facing a bet of 300 into a pot of 600 on the turn. My hand is Ace-high, which is currently weak against any pair, but the price (pot odds of 3:1) gives me an incentive to call. Given the board texture (7d-5s-4d-Kh), there are many draws that Flash might be betting, and my Ace has some theoretical equity. Folding seems too tight here given the pot odds and the likelihood of the opponent betting a wide range of hands from the small blind. Calling allows me to reach the river and re-evaluate if the opponent checks or continues betting.

I have top pair with a weak kicker. The river card (2h) is largely a blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. After betting the turn and getting called, checking the river is a prudent move to control the pot size. I am likely ahead of the opponent's middle pairs or missed diamond draws, but I am behind any King with a better kicker or a slow-played monster. Checking allows me to reach showdown cheaply or potentially induce a bluff from a missed draw.

I am in the Big Blind and the opponent has checked the river. My hand, Ace-high, has very limited showdown value on this board (7d-5s-4d-Kh-2h), as the turn call from the opponent suggests they have at least a pair or a draw that may have hit. Attempting to bet as a bluff here offers very little fold equity, as a calling range for the turn bet likely contains pairs or better. Checking behind is the most prudent strategy to reach showdown without risking additional chips with a losing hand.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Q8o. This is a standard opening hand for a steal attempt from this position. I have shown aggression in previous hands by raising to 300 (3x) from the button/small blind and will maintain that sizing to put pressure on the blinds.

I am in the small blind with 9-7 offsuit, which is a weak hand. Facing a button raise, calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal holding, and 3-betting is not advisable with this hand strength. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play to conserve my stack.

A6o is a weak offsuit Ace. Facing a 3x open from the Button while out of position in the Big Blind, calling will often lead to difficult spots post-flop due to reverse implied odds when an Ace hits, as we will frequently be outkicked. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve chips for better spots.

I am on the button with 9-8 offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a reasonable hand to open with from the button to steal the blinds or take the initiative. A 3x open (300 chips) is consistent with the table's previous betting patterns and puts appropriate pressure on the small and big blinds.

A4o is too weak to call or 3-bet out of position from the small blind against a button open, especially with the big blind still left to act. The best decision is to fold and conserve the stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with K2o. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds (200 to win a total pot of 650), K2o is a very weak hand to defend out of position, especially against a 3x raise. It realization of equity post-flop is difficult, and I often find myself dominated if I hit a King or simply folding to a continuation bet if I miss. Given it is early in the tournament and I have a healthy stack, I will choose the conservative route and fold this marginal hand.

I am first to act on the Button with Jack-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak hand and falls far outside of any reasonable opening range. The best move is to fold and save my stack for better opportunities.

In a 3-handed game, KQo is a premium starting hand. Since it has been folded to me in the Small Blind, I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on the Big Blind. This sizing is consistent with my previous opening raises and allows me to take control of the hand with a range advantage.

Jack-three offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against a raise from the small blind. While the pot odds might seem tempting, defending with such a dominated hand is not a profitable long-term strategy. I will conserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. While 3-handed play warrants a wide opening range from the button, J7o is at the lower end of that range. Given that I have been quite active in previous hands with several 3x raises, I will choose to fold this hand to maintain a balanced image and avoid playing a marginal pot out of position or against potentially stronger defending ranges from the blinds.

I am holding pocket queens, which is a premium hand. With the button having folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. It is optimal to raise to build the pot and extract value, as checking or just calling from the small blind would allow the big blind to realize their equity too cheaply. A raise to 350 chips is a solid sizing to pressure the big blind and define the range.

Opponent raised to 3.5x from the small blind. K4o is a weak hand with poor playability post-flop, especially out of position if I call. Folding is the best choice here to preserve chips for a better spot.

In a 3-handed game, being on the button provides a significant positional advantage. Holding 9-10 offsuit is a perfectly acceptable hand to open from this position to steal the blinds and potentially take control of the pot. A standard opening raise size of 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate to apply pressure while maintaining a disciplined stack management strategy.

Facing a 3bb raise from the Button while in the Small Blind, K5 suited is too weak to call or 3-bet for value. We will be out of position post-flop, and our King is often dominated by the Button's opening range. The safest and most +EV play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

In a 3-handed game, Ten-Eight offsuit is a standard defending hand from the Big Blind against a Button open. I am getting 2.25:1 pot odds to call (200 more to win a total pot of 450). While I will be playing out of position for the rest of the hand, folding here would be too tight and allow the Button to raise profitably with a very wide range. I will call and see a flop.

On this flop of Ace-Queen-Four with two hearts, my Ten-Eight offsuit has missed completely. I have no pair and no significant draw. As the first to act, checking is the standard play to see how the aggressor reacts. I do not want to invest more chips into this pot without any equity.

The board (Qh As 4h) is a high-card board that hits my opening range, but my hand (9-10 offsuit) has completely missed, offering no pair and negligible drawing equity. Since the big blind checked to me, there is no immediate pressure, and bloating the pot as a bluff on this texture is risky. Checking back is the optimal move to preserve my stack, maintain pot control, and see a free turn card where I can re-evaluate based on the next community card.

On the turn, the board is AQ49. After calling pre-flop with Ten-Eight offsuit and checking the flop, I see that the aggressor (Flashlight) also checked back the flop. This check-back suggests he likely does not have a strong Ace or Queen, though he could be pot-controlling a medium pair or a draw. My hand has no showdown value and no significant draws. While I could attempt a bluff here to take down the 650 pot, checking is the most disciplined play. If Flashlight checks again, I get a free look at the river; if he bets, I can comfortably fold knowing I have no equity.

On a board of A-Q-4-9, I have a pair of nines. This is a weak made hand, currently losing to any Ace or Queen, and even a pair of fours is ahead of me if the kicker is better, though likely I am currently behind a significant portion of the calling range. Since Flash checked, checking behind is the optimal move to maintain pot control, avoid bloating the pot against a potentially stronger hand, and realize my showdown value cheaply.

On the river, the board completes a possible flush with the 5 of hearts. I have 10-high (10h 8c), which holds zero showdown value. The aggressor, Flashlight, has checked back on both the flop and the turn, which indicates significant weakness or a hand with marginal showdown value like a small pair or a weak Queen. While I could attempt a bluff to represent the flush or a slow-played Ace, I have no specific read on Flashlight's tendency to fold to river leads. Checking is the most disciplined and lowest-risk play here; if Flashlight checks back, I lose the pot but preserve my stack, and if he bets, I can comfortably fold as I have no equity.

The board is very wet with three hearts, an Ace, and a Queen, making it extremely likely that my opponent has connected with the board if they have any heart, Ace, or Queen. My pair of nines is a marginal showdown hand that is unlikely to be the best hand if I were to bet and get called, as I would only be value-betting into hands that beat me. Checking behind allows me to see a showdown for free and avoid potentially losing more chips in a spot where I am essentially bluff-catching.
J2 offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even on the button. Folding pre-flop is the mathematically sound and standard play here to conserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 2h, which is an extremely weak hand. After the button folds, it is just me and the Big Blind. While there is an opportunity to steal or complete the blind, 92o is not worth the risk of playing out of position against Flashlight. I will fold to conserve chips for a better starting hand.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight suited, which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. I will make a standard 3x raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability and equity. This sizing is consistent with my previous opening raises.

Holding 6d-Td in the small blind against a button raise is unfavorable. Playing out of position with a marginal, disconnected hand will likely lead to difficult post-flop decisions and potential chip bleeding. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term play here.
My hand, 75o, is a very weak offsuit gapper. Playing it out of position against a pre-flop raiser is unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the best choice here to conserve my stack.
Nine-three suited is a very weak hand with low equity. In this tournament stage, it is not worth committing chips to play such a marginal holding. Folding is the standard, disciplined choice.

I am dealt 8s 2d, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected starting hand. Even though I am in the Small Blind and it's a blind-versus-blind situation, playing this hand out of position is highly unprofitable. The best action is to fold and save my chips for a better opportunity.

A9o is a strong enough starting hand to open-raise from the button in a 3-handed game. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the small and big blinds to defend with marginal hands, and it gives me the betting lead with positional advantage post-flop if called.

I have 4s 2s in the small blind. Pro raised to 300 from the button. While 42s is suited, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. I would need significant equity or a better hand to justify calling here.

Facing a raise from the button, my holding of 6h-3h is too weak to profitably defend from the big blind. Calling would put me in an out-of-position scenario with a marginal hand that struggles to connect with boards well enough to justify the call. The best course of action is to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with Q6s in a 3-handed game. This is a strong enough hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and appropriate for this position.

Holding 4h-3d offsuit is a very weak starting hand. Calling the raise of 300 from the small blind would put me out of position against the button and the big blind. Folding is the standard, disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Flash is opening to 3bb frequently from the Button. As the Big Blind in a 3-handed game, our defending range against a Button open should be relatively wide. 97s is a suited one-gapper that has reasonable equity and playability post-flop against a wide range. The pot odds dictate we need about 31% equity, which 97s easily has against Flash's likely wide BTN raising range. Calling to see a flop is the standard and best play here.

I flopped an open-ended straight draw (9-8-7-6-5 or 10-9-8-7-6) on a J-8-6 board. Acting first against the pre-flop raiser, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. If Flash bets, I will have a strong draw to either call or potentially check-raise depending on the sizing. For now, checking controls the pot and allows me to evaluate their action.

On a flop of Jd-8h-6d, I have bottom pair with a Queen kicker. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture hits the big blind's defending range fairly well (containing many combinations of 8s, Jacks, and straight draws). Checking back here allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and potentially induce a bluff or reach a cheap showdown. Betting risks a check-raise that would force me to fold my pair.

The turn card gives me middle pair (Nines) to go with my open-ended straight draw (any 10 or 5 completes it). Since the flop checked through, Flash's range is likely weak or capped at a marginal made hand. Leading out here builds the pot in case I hit my strong draw, extracts value from weaker draws, and protects my hand by folding out overcards that still have decent equity. A bet of around half-pot (350) accomplishes these goals effectively.

I have bottom pair (Sixes) with a Queen kicker. The turn Nine is a very coordinated card that completes several potential straights (any Seven or Ten) and hits many hands in the Big Blind's defending range. Pro led out for 350 into a 650 pot (making the total pot 1000). I am getting roughly 3:1 pot odds to call. Given that I have position and the bet is relatively small compared to my stack, I will call once to see the river, but I must remain cautious as my hand is weak on this board texture.

The river card completes both the flush draw and a four-card straight on the board (8-9-T-J). While my 7 makes a Jack-high straight, it is the bottom end of the straight since any Queen makes a higher straight. Additionally, the third diamond arrived, meaning any potential flush draw got there. Given how many hands in Flash's range now beat me (any Q, any flush), I have no value bet here. Checking to control the pot and evaluating Flash's action is the safest and most optimal play.

I have completed a Queen-high straight on the river (8-9-10-J-Q). However, the board is highly coordinated with three diamonds present, making a flush possible, and any King in Pro's hand would form a higher straight. Since Pro checked, I will check back to realize my equity and win the pot safely, avoiding a potential check-raise on such a dangerous board texture.

With Ace-Nine suited in position on the button in a three-handed game, this is a premium hand to open with. I want to take the initiative and pressure the blinds to either steal the pot or set up a post-flop advantage. A raise to 300 is a standard, disciplined open that maintains consistency with my previous playstyle.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 2s, which is arguably the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The Button has already raised to 300. There is no justification to call or raise with such weak cards out of position. The optimal and only sensible play is to fold.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind. In a three-handed game, this is a very strong starting hand, especially against a Button open. Flashlight has been opening frequently. I will put in a 3-bet to 1000 to take control of the hand, build the pot for value, and potentially end the hand pre-flop. If called, I will be out of position but with a strong range advantage.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind (Flash) while holding Ace-Nine suited, I have a strong enough hand to continue but not enough to justify a 4-bet. Flash has shown aggression throughout the session, which could mean they are polarizing their range. By calling the 700-chip difference, I maintain my position, keep the pot manageable, and see a flop with a hand that has good post-flop potential. This is a disciplined approach to a 3-bet in a 3-handed game.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on an Ace-high board after 3-betting pre-flop. I have a range advantage here and want to continue the aggression. Betting about 1/3 of the pot is a standard continuation bet that extracts value from weaker Aces, Queens, and middle pairs while keeping the pot controlled and charging any potential draws.

I am holding Top Pair (Ace) with a Nine kicker on an Ace-high board. While my kicker is vulnerable, folding Top Pair to a single bet after 3-betting pre-flop is too weak, especially given my pot odds and stack size. Flash is an aggressive player, and this could be a continuation bet with a wider range than just premium hands like AQ or AK. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, see a turn card, and re-evaluate without bloating the pot against a potentially stronger range.

The four of hearts on the turn completes a possible flush draw and creates potential for two-pair combinations like A4 or Q4. Having 3-bet pre-flop and continuation-bet the flop, I have established a strong range, but with top pair, Jack kicker (AJ), I am now in a marginal spot for value-betting on such a wet board. Checking is a prudent pot-control measure that allows me to see Flashlight's reaction, potentially catch a bluff from missed draws, and prevent the pot from ballooning against hands that now beat me, such as flushes or two-pairs.

I am holding Top Pair (Aces) with a Nine kicker and a flush draw on a board showing three hearts (Ah, Qh, 4h). My opponent, Flash, checked to me on the turn after leading the flop. This check could signal a desire to pot-control with a medium-strength hand or potentially trap. By betting now, I gain value from mid-pairs or weaker Aces while applying pressure and potentially forcing a fold. If I am called, I still have significant equity with my flush draw to improve on the river.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker (AJ) on a board that now contains three hearts. I will call to see the river and evaluate the final action, keeping the pot manageable while not folding a strong made hand to a single bet.

On the river, the board now shows Ace-high with three hearts and a King. I have top pair with a Jack kicker (AJ). The King on the river is a scare card that could have improved hands like AK or KQ in Flashlight's range, and the flush remains a significant threat. Betting here would likely only get called by hands that beat me (flushes, two pairs like AQ/AK/KQ, or sets) and fold out hands I currently beat. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play to reach showdown as cheaply as possible or to potentially induce a bluff from missed straight draws (like JT or 56s).

I am holding top pair with an Ace, but with a weak Nine kicker. The board is Ah-7c-Qh-4h-Kd. While my pair of Aces beats K-x and Q-x hands, it loses to any two-pair combination (like A-Q, A-7, or K-Q) and any Ace with a better kicker. Given the opponent called my turn bet, they have shown strength. Betting now would likely only be called by hands that have me beat, while checking allows me to realize my showdown value against the range of weaker made hands they might be holding. This is a disciplined check-back to secure a showdown.

A2o is a very weak Ace that plays poorly post-flop. J6o is a weak hand to play from the Small Blind. Being out of position against the Big Blind with a hand that has poor connectivity and post-flop potential makes folding the most prudent decision to avoid losing chips in a marginal spot.

I am on the button with T6s in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand for a button raise to put pressure on the blinds and utilize positional advantage. I will raise to my standard sizing of 300 chips.

I am in the small blind holding 8c 4h, which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the button (Flash), calling is not mathematically sound as I would be playing out of position with a dominated hand. Preserving my stack is the priority at this stage of the tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-2 offsuit facing a 3x raise from the Button. This is the worst starting hand in No-Limit Texas Hold'em and mathematically unprofitable to play, so the correct decision is an easy fold.

I have a suited Ace (Ad8d) in position on the button, which is a strong starting hand to open with. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot or play the hand in position.

Q8 offsuit is a weak hand to play from the Small Blind, especially facing a 3x open from the Button. We will be out of position post-flop if we call, and we are vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind who has a large stack. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play here.

I am in the big blind with 2c 4h, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Flashlight raised to 300 from the button. I am dealt 83o on the button, which is an extremely weak and unplayable hand in this format. The standard play is to fold pre-flop to avoid committing chips with negative expected value.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 6h. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and Flashlight in the Big Blind. I am on the button with 7c 5d, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have the chip lead and positional advantage, this hand is too weak to justify a raise or a call against the small and big blinds who have shown willingness to play. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 6-4 offsuit, a very weak starting hand. With no action from the Button, I must decide whether to complete the small blind to see a flop or fold. Given that I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand with a weak range that is easily dominated by the Big Blind's range, folding is the most disciplined and stack-efficient move.

3c 8s is a very weak hand with no playability or high-card strength. In a 3-handed game, this hand should be folded pre-flop from any position as it has poor equity against the likely opening ranges of my opponents.

With A6o in the Small Blind and the Button folding, this is a prime blind-versus-blind situation. A6o is ahead of a large portion of the Big Blind's defending range. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) is standard to either pick up the dead money immediately or to take the betting initiative into the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d 2h, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Ah7h is a very strong hand to open with from the button in a 3-handed game. I'll raise to 3 times the big blind to build the pot, take the initiative, and potentially win the blinds right now. This is a standard opening size.

I have J8s in the Small Blind. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard 3x raise. J8s is a reasonable speculative hand that has good playability post-flop, even out of position. Given the pot odds (calling 250 to win a total pot that will be 700 or more), this is a standard defending call to see a flop.

My hand is [3h, 2c], which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Hold'em. Despite the price being relatively cheap to call (200 chips to win a pot of 700), calling with such a range-dominated hand out of position against a button raiser and a small blind caller is negative expected value. There is no strategic reason to invest chips here with essentially zero equity, so I will fold.

I have a very strong draw on this board with both a flush draw (diamonds) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7 to complete a 4-5-6-7-8 straight). Being out of position as the Small Blind against the pre-flop raiser, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see their bet sizing and decide whether to call or check-raise to maximize the value of my high equity.

The 4-5-6 flop heavily favors the pre-flop caller's range, which includes many sets, two pairs, and straight combinations. Although I have an open-ended straight draw and an Ace overcard, making a continuation bet here could easily induce a check-raise and put me in a difficult position. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity for free, and safely see the turn.

I currently hold a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw, giving me significant equity in this hand. On the flop, the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked back, which suggests they might be weak or playing a marginal hand cautiously. By checking again on the turn, I am giving Pro the opportunity to bluff or bet a medium-strength hand, while ensuring I can see the river card as cheaply as possible to realize my equity. If Pro bets, I have a very comfortable call given the pot odds and my number of outs.

With the opponent checking again on the turn, it's a prime opportunity for a delayed continuation bet. I have an open-ended straight draw (any 3 or 8 gives me a straight) and an Ace overcard, giving me strong equity even if called. Betting here allows me to potentially take down the pot right now by folding out hands with better high-card value, while also building the pot in case I hit my straight on the river.

I have a strong semi-bluffing hand with both a flush draw (9 outs) and a gutshot straight draw (3 additional outs for the 7). This gives me approximately 26% equity. Pro bet 350 into a 700 pot, giving me pot odds of 3-to-1, or exactly 25%. Since my equity is slightly higher than the odds required, and I have significant implied odds if I hit my flush or straight on the river, a call is the most mathematically sound and profitable play here.

I have improved to top pair with the Jack on the river. However, the board is extremely coordinated with multiple straight possibilities (any 3 or 7 makes a straight). After calling a bet on the turn with a flush draw and a gutshot, hitting the Jack is a decent result, but I am out of position. Checking is the most prudent play here to avoid bloating the pot against a potentially better hand while giving my opponent the opportunity to check back or bluff with missed draws.

I missed my straight draw and ended up with Ace-high on a 4-5-6-2-J board. The opponent check-called my turn bet, indicating they likely have at least a marginal pair or a draw. Since I checked the flop, it is hard to credibly represent a very strong value hand that would force the opponent to fold a pair. Checking behind is the safest option, allowing me to get to a free showdown and sometimes win against busted draws without risking more chips.

I am on the button with Jh 8c. In a 3-handed game, this hand is strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down pre-flop. Q7 offsuit is a weak hand to play from the small blind, especially against a button opening raise. Being out of position makes it difficult to realize equity post-flop. Flash is raising frequently from the Button. With a pair of sixes in the Big Blind, we have a decent hand to set-mine or play on low boards. Our stacks are deep enough to justify calling for implied odds, and playing a smaller pot out of position with a low pocket pair is standard.

With pocket sixes on a 3-4-Q board, I have a vulnerable middle pair. Acting first out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser to evaluate his action. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot and see if he continuation bets before committing more chips.

I raised pre-flop and have the initiative. The flop contains a Queen, which hits my range better than the Big Blind's defending range. By checking back, I control the pot size and keep the option open to bluff or realize equity on the river depending on Pro's action and the final card.

The Ace of clubs on the river completes potential flush draws and hits any Ace-high hands my opponent might have checked behind on the turn. Given my substantial chip lead and the risk that Pro has improved to an Ace or a flush, I will check back and concede the pot if I am beaten, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the button position with J5 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand that does not warrant an investment. I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded. While it's only 50 chips to call, J6o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. 94o is an extremely weak starting hand. Even with the advantage of the Button position, it falls far outside any profitable opening range. Folding is the only sensible choice.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, a very strong hand for a blind-vs-blind scenario. The Button has folded. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and put pressure on the Big Blind, which has been my standard opening size in previous hands.

I am in the big blind with King-Ten offsuit, which is a strong enough hand to defend against a steal attempt from the small blind. I am on the button with a pocket pair of threes. In a three-handed game, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand and warrants a raise to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately. A standard raise to 300 has been my consistent opening size and remains appropriate here.

I am in the small blind with King-Four offsuit, which is a weak hand. Playing this out of position against a button raise is suboptimal and would put me in a difficult spot post-flop. K2s is a weak suited king. Against a 3x open from the button, defending out of position is marginal and can lead to dominated spots if I hit a King. The pot odds are okay, but the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make folding the safest option to preserve my stack.

I am dealt pocket Aces, which is the best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My goal is to build a large pot, isolate opponents, and gain value early. Given the standard open raise size observed in previous hands, a raise to 300 is an appropriate sizing to achieve these goals.
With 6s 7h offsuit in the small blind facing a 3x raise from the button, my hand is too weak to play. The best decision is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

On the flop of 8d 2h 3h, I have Queen-high. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, Flashlight. This board is relatively dry, although it does offer some backdoor possibilities. I am in the big blind facing a button open. With K7o, I am priced in to call a raise of 200 into a pot of 450. Defending the big blind with K-high is standard procedure against a wide button opening range, as I have sufficient pot odds to realize my equity.

Pro checked the flop of Td-2h-5h. With King-high and no pair or significant draw, I have minimal equity against a range that likely includes Broadway cards or pocket pairs. With Ace-Queen high on a T-5-2 board with a flush draw, we completely missed but still have two strong overcards. The board shows Td-2h-5h-3c. I hold Ks-7d, which is only King-high with no pair or flush draw. The turn 3c makes a possible straight on the board for anyone holding a 4. With Ace-Queen high and a newly added gutshot to the wheel, checking behind again allows me to realize my equity for free. I am in the Big Blind with King-high (Ks-7d). The board of Td-2h-5h-3c-2s has been checked down completely by both players. Flashlight has checked to me on all three streets. My Ace-Queen high has solid showdown value against missed flush draws (like Jh9h or KhJh) or missed straight draws. I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. While I have a large stack and have been active, this hand is too weak to open with even from the button, especially since the opponents have shown a tendency to play back or 3-bet. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

Q7o is a weak offsuit hand, and we are out of position in the small blind facing a raise from the button. The best action is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Jh 9s is a weak, unsuited hand that performs poorly out of position. While the pot odds offer a reasonable price to call, the lack of equity and the disadvantage of being out of position post-flop make folding the most disciplined choice against an aggressive opponent.

Holding 97o on the button is a weak offsuit connector/gapper. It doesn't have enough playability or equity to justify an open-raise or a call, especially when facing blinds that can easily 3-bet or defend. The most profitable action is to simply fold.

We completely missed this flop, possessing only King-high and no meaningful draws. Facing a continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser with a player still to act behind us, continuing in the hand would be a purely speculative play with poor odds. Folding is the correct choice to preserve our stack.

I am in the small blind holding pocket eights on a paired, dry board of nine-nine-four. As the pre-flop caller, I am out of position and my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher. I am in the small blind with pocket eights on a board of 9-9-4-3. 6cTh is a weak, unsuited hand with poor connectivity. Even on the button, there is no strategic value in playing this holding against a full field. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play to preserve my 58.5 BB stack for better opportunities.

Folded to me in the Small Blind. I hold A3 suited, which is a strong hand for a Blind vs Blind encounter. I will raise to 3x the big blind to steal the pot or play a heads-up pot with the initiative and a good suited Ace.

Since the button folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. King-Seven offsuit is a strong enough hand to raise for value and to attempt to take down the blinds immediately. I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten suited. The Small Blind (Flash) has raised to 300. With 5750 chips and pot odds of 2:1 (calling 200 to win a 400 pot), calling is a standard and profitable play with suited broadway cards. I will call the 200 to see the flop.
