On the turn 5s, the board is now 4d-Th-3h-5s. I bet the flop with King-high and was called by Flashlight. The 5s is a card that could have potentially improved Flashlight's range if they were calling with a straight draw (like 67 or A2) or a small pair. Since my hand hasn't improved and I am out of position, checking is the most cautious play to control the pot size and see how Flashlight reacts. If Flashlight checks back, I might still have the best hand or can see a cheap river. If they bet, I will likely fold as I have very little equity.

I am sitting with top pair (tens) on a board that now features a straight draw (2-6-7). The turn card 5s completes a straight for 6-7 and gives additional equity to various draws. Flash checked to me, signaling weakness or potentially a trap with a made straight or set. Since I have decent showdown value but not the nuts, checking behind is the best way to control the pot size and reach a cheap showdown, while avoiding bloating a pot against a range that may have connected better with the turn card.

The river Ace of hearts is a scare card that completes a potential flush and several straight possibilities (any deuce). Having checked the turn to control the pot, I have essentially given up the lead. My King-high has almost no showdown value, but bluffing on this board texture into an opponent who called the flop is high-risk, especially since Flashlight's stack is relatively short. I will check and hope for a check-back, though I expect to fold if faced with a significant bet.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of Tens (Qc, Tc) on a board of 4d, Th, 3h, 5s, Ah. The Ace on the river is a significant card, as it devalues my pair of Tens significantly, making them middle pair. Flash checked the turn and the river, which suggests a weak hand or a missed draw, but betting here is unlikely to induce calls from worse hands and could be trapped by better hands that were slow-playing. Given the board texture and the action, checking behind to see the showdown is the safest and most optimal play.

I am on the button with King-Seven offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. My stack is the largest at the table, allowing me to play aggressively. I will raise to 300, which has been the consistent opening size in this tournament.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand, Queen-Six offsuit. Playing out of position against a button raise with such a hand is generally unprofitable, as it is difficult to navigate post-flop with low connectivity and poor kicker potential. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic decision to preserve my stack.

I am dealt 73 offsuit in the Big Blind, which is an extremely weak and unplayable hand. The Button raised to 3 big blinds. Even though I am getting some pot odds to defend my blind, 73o has terrible playability post-flop and almost no equity against a standard Button opening range. The only optimal choice here is to fold.

With Queen-Jack suited on the button, I have a strong positional advantage and a hand that plays well in raised pots. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or build a pot in position.

I am in the Small Blind with A4o facing a 3x open from the Button. While an Ace is a good blocker, A4o is a poor hand to play out of position because it struggles to make strong top pairs and lacks flush potential. A 3-bet bluff is possible but A4 suited is a much better candidate for that. Calling leads to difficult post-flop spots out of position. The most solid and standard play here is to simply fold and save chips for a better spot.

I hold Ace-King of clubs in the big blind, a premium starting hand. The button (Flashlight) has opened for 300. Given that I am out of position and have a significant chip lead over Flashlight, I will put in a 3-bet to 1,000 to build value and take initiative in the hand. This size is roughly 3.3x the opening raise, which is standard when playing out of position.

Facing a 3-bet from the big blind after my initial raise, QJs is a premium hand that plays very well post-flop. While 4-betting is an option, my stack depth of 61BB makes calling and playing in position the more balanced and disciplined approach, allowing me to realize my equity and evaluate the board texture.

I hold Ace-King of clubs on a Ten-Jack-Five rainbow board after 3-betting pre-flop. While the board is somewhat coordinated and hits a typical 3-bet calling range (containing hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, or pocket pairs), I maintain the range advantage and betting lead. A continuation bet of approximately one-third of the pot (700) is a standard play here to exert pressure on the opponent's air and marginal hands while keeping the pot manageable in case I am raised or called and fail to improve on the turn.

I have top pair (Jacks) with a strong Queen kicker on a rainbow board. Flash, who is aggressive and 3-bet me pre-flop, continuation-bet into me. With a board of T-J-5, I am ahead of many of their bluffs (like A-K or lower pairs) and semi-bluffs. Raising would likely force them to fold their bluffs while only being called by hands that crush me (like sets or better top pairs). Calling keeps their range wide and allows me to control the pot size while realizing my equity.

The turn Ace of Diamonds is a double-edged sword. While it provides me with Top Pair, Top Kicker (TPTK) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen), it is also a card that completes the Broadway straight for any King-Queen (KQ) combination, which is well within Flashlight's range for calling a 3-bet. Given that the pot is already quite large relative to Flashlight's remaining stack (3450 pot vs 4700 stack), I will check this turn. This allows me to control the pot size, evaluate Flashlight's reaction to the Ace, and potentially induce a bluff from weaker hands or draws while maintaining the ability to call a reasonable bet.

The board has become quite dangerous with the Ace of Diamonds on the turn. My pair of Jacks is now likely behind any Ace in Flash's 3-betting range, such as AK or AQ. Flash checking the turn after betting the flop could indicate they have hit the Ace and are trapping, or that they are checking a hand that missed the turn. Given my position, checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot, maintain my showdown value, and see a free river card. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands and will likely only get called by better hands.

I have Top Pair, Top Kicker with Ace-King. The river Trey of Diamonds completes a potential backdoor flush and the board already contained a potential straight with King-Queen. After checking the turn to control the pot, I will check the river as well. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands given the texture, but checking allows Flashlight to bluff with missed draws or check back with marginal made hands like a Jack or a weaker Ace. This is a standard pot control line when the board is this coordinated.

Flash checked the river on a board of T-J-5-A-3 with three diamonds. My pair of Jacks has showdown value, but it is unlikely to be the best hand if Flash has an Ace or a set of 3s, 5s, or a disguised flush. Since Flash checked the turn and the river, checking back is the optimal play to realize my showdown value without bloating a pot that I may lose, especially since I am unlikely to get called by worse hands.

53s is too weak to open from the button 3-handed, especially with an aggressive big stack in the small blind and a shorter stack in the big blind who might shove. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

I have Ace-King suited, one of the strongest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With the Button folding, I am head-up against the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on Flashlight, who has a shorter stack. This sizing is consistent with previous action in the tournament and represents a strong range.

Flash has raised from the small blind, and I am in the big blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. Given the pot odds of 2-to-1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), defending with this hand is profitable against an aggressive opponent who is opening a wide range. Calling allows me to see a flop and evaluate further.

On a coordinated board like 10h-9s-7s, my Ace-King overcards are vulnerable and the texture heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many middling connectors, pairs, and straight draws. While I have the range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor, betting into this specific board is risky as I'm likely to get called or raised by many hands that currently beat me. Checking allows me to see a turn card for free and control the pot size, keeping my options open depending on the turn card and Flashlight's action.

I am in the big blind and have hit middle pair with my Nine of Hearts. The flop of Ten-Nine-Seven (with two spades) is quite coordinated, offering several straight and flush draw possibilities for my opponent. Flash checked to me. Checking back is the prudent play here; it allows me to control the size of the pot, keep my range balanced, and realize my showdown equity without bloating the pot against potential draws or stronger hands that might check-raise me. I will check to see the turn card.

The turn card (4d) is a complete blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. After checking the flop and seeing my opponent check back, it's possible they are holding a marginal made hand or a draw. While my Ace-King still has six outs to potentially become the best hand, the board remains highly coordinated (T-9-7), which likely connects with many hands in the Big Blind's defending range. Betting now might force out pure air, but any pair or decent draw will likely call. By checking again, I maintain pot control, avoid being check-raised off my equity, and keep the option to see a river card for free or evaluate a small bet from Flashlight.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of Nines on a coordinated board of Ten-Nine-Seven-Four. My opponent, Flash, has checked the flop and now the turn, which likely indicates weakness, a draw, or a cautious playing style. While my pair of Nines is a reasonable hand, it is primarily a bluff-catcher at this point. Betting might only force folds from hands I am already beating or induce a check-raise from hands that have me beat. Checking back allows me to maintain a controlled pot size and reach a cheap showdown, which is the most prudent strategy for my marginal pair.

The river pairs the Ten, which is a relatively neutral card. Throughout the hand, Flashlight has checked alongside me, suggesting they likely hold either a weak made hand or a missed draw. Ace-King has significant showdown value in this spot against missed straight draws (like 8-6 or 6-5) and missed spade flush draws. While I could attempt to bluff out a weak pair of 7s or 4s, it is more likely that checking will allow me to win the pot at showdown without risking more chips against a random pair that refuses to fold. I will check and take the hand to showdown.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of Nines, giving me two pair (Tens and Nines) on this board. My opponent, Flash, has checked down every street (flop, turn, and river), which strongly suggests they have either a complete air hand or a very weak made hand that they are trying to get to showdown with. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands, as any hand I beat will likely fold to a bet, and betting could potentially induce a check-raise if they were slow-playing a Ten. Therefore, checking back to reach a cheap showdown is the most optimal and risk-averse play to secure the pot.

I am on the button with K5o. In a three-handed game, any King is typically strong enough to open for a raise from the button to put pressure on the blinds, especially since I have a significant chip lead. A standard 3x raise (300 chips) is a consistent play for me and allows me to take control of the pot early or win it pre-flop.

I am in the small blind with 7c 6c, which is a playable suited connector hand. Given the raise to 300 from the button (Flash), calling 250 more into a pot that will be at least 700 is mathematically acceptable, especially with potential for implied odds if I hit a strong flop. Folding here is too conservative, and raising is not optimal given the risk of running into a stronger hand against a very large stack.

T2o is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Even with pot odds from the big blind, playing this trash out of position multi-way against a raiser and a caller is extremely unprofitable. An easy fold.

I hold 7c 6c on a board of Kh 4c 9c, giving me a flush draw. Being out of position against the button, checking is the standard play to control the pot size while seeing a turn card. If the button bets, I will re-evaluate based on pot odds to determine if chasing the flush is mathematically sound.

On the flop of Kh-4c-9c, I have top pair with a King. I was the pre-flop aggressor. Flashlight checked to me. While my kicker (5) is weak, I should continuation bet here to protect my hand from draws (especially flush draws and straight draws like 5-6 or 7-8) and to get value from weaker pairs or draws. A bet of 350 (half the pot) is a standard sizing to accomplish this while maintaining control of the pot size.

I hold a flush draw with 7c 6c on a board of Kh 4c 9c. The pot is 1050 and the bet is 350, offering me 3:1 pot odds (25% required equity). With 9 outs to hit the flush on the turn, my direct equity is approximately 19%. Given the significant implied odds against a larger stack, calling is the most strategic play here to see the turn and potentially win a larger pot.

I have completed a flush on the turn with 7c 6c on a board of Kh 4c 9c 3c. The pot is currently 1400. Since I am first to act on this street, I should lead out with a value bet. A bet of 700 (half pot) is appropriate to gain value from opponents holding a King, straight draws, or pair-plus-draw combinations, without charging too much to scare them away.

On the turn, the third club completes potential flushes. Flashlight has led out with a bet of 700 into a pot of 1400 after calling my flop continuation bet. While this 'donk bet' often signals strength (like a flush, a set, or two pair), I still hold top pair with Kings. My kicker is weak, but folding top pair to a single bet when getting 3:1 pot odds is too conservative in a three-handed game. Flashlight could be betting a weaker King, a lower pair with a club kicker, or a straight draw. I will call the 700 to see the river and evaluate based on the final card and my opponent's action.

I have successfully made a flush with my 7c 6c on a board of Kh 4c 9c 3c 4s. I am first to act on the river. Given the strength of my hand, a value bet is the optimal strategy. A bet of 700 represents a significant but reasonable portion of the pot, designed to extract value from holdings like a King or lower flush draws, without overcommitting if the opponent holds a higher flush or has rivered a full house with a 4.

I hold top pair (Kings) with a 5 kicker. The board shows three clubs, which makes a flush possible, and the river paired the 4. Flashlight led on the turn and followed up with a 700-chip bet on the river into a 2800-chip pot. This 25% pot-sized bet often functions as a blocking bet from a weaker King, a pair of nines, or even a missed draw trying to buy a cheap showdown. Given that I am getting 5:1 pot odds (calling 700 to win 4200), I only need to have the best hand roughly 17% of the time to make this call profitable. In a three-handed game, folding top pair to such a small bet is too exploitable. I will call.

Being on the button in a 3-handed game gives me a strong positional advantage. Ace-Seven offsuit is a premium enough hand in this short-handed scenario to open the action and attempt to steal the blinds or take initiative in the pot. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard, aggressive opening size to accomplish this.

K8o is a weak offsuit hand, and playing it from the Small Blind against a Button raise means I'll be out of position for the entire hand with a marginal holding. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Queen of hearts, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Flashlight has opened from the button for 300. I have the chip lead and want to put pressure on the smaller stacks while building a pot with a very strong range. A 3-bet here is standard for value and protection. I will raise to 1000, which is consistent with my previous 3-bet sizing in this tournament (as seen in hands like #18 and #72).

Flash has 3-bet my button open to 1000. While As7d is a marginal hand that can be dominated by stronger Aces, I have position and it's a 3-handed game, where ranges are wider. The pot odds are acceptable to call and see a flop, and I want to avoid folding too easily to aggression. Calling is a balanced way to proceed in position.

On this flop of Ts-Jd-6s, I have two overcards and a backdoor straight draw with my Ace-Queen of hearts. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage, particularly in higher-card combinations. A continuation bet here is appropriate to maintain initiative and potentially take the pot down immediately or set up a turn play. I will bet around 35% of the pot, which is enough to put pressure on Flashlight's middling hands while keeping the pot size manageable if I am called.

The opponent has made a small bet of approximately 25% of the pot on a board of Ten-Jack-Six. With Ace-High and a backdoor straight draw, I have sufficient pot odds to continue in position. Folding is too weak given the price and my positional advantage, while raising would likely be overplaying a marginal holding against a 3-bettor's range. Calling is the most disciplined way to keep the pot controlled and evaluate the turn.

On the turn 7c, the board is becoming increasingly coordinated (Ts-Jd-6s-7c). Flashlight called both my pre-flop 3-bet and my flop continuation bet, suggesting a range that likely includes one-pair hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, AT, or middle pairs like 88 and 99. The 7c doesn't help my Ace-Queen, and given Flashlight's remaining stack of 5,425 relative to the 3,500 pot, a bet here might commit me to the hand or invite a shove that I cannot comfortably call with Ace-high. I will check to control the size of the pot and see how Flashlight reacts, potentially taking a free card to the river.

I hold a pair of sevens on a board of Ten-Jack-Six-Seven. The opponent, who showed aggression pre-flop and on the flop, has checked to me on the turn. My pair of sevens has some showdown value, but it is a marginal hand against a 3-betting range. Betting would likely isolate me against better hands like Jacks or Tens, or force out hands that I am currently beating. Checking behind is the most prudent strategy to control the pot, reach the river, and potentially realize my equity without overcommitting to a potentially dominated hand.

The river 5h is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. After I checked the turn and Flashlight checked behind, his range likely consists of marginal made hands like weak Jacks (QJ, KJ), Tens (T9s, AT), or middle pairs (99, 88) that are looking for a cheap showdown. My Ace-high has some showdown value against his potential missed draws like KQ or air, but it's unlikely to be the best hand. Betting as a bluff here is risky because Flashlight has already called two streets and might be unwilling to fold a pair in a 3-handed game. I will check to see the showdown for free and minimize potential losses.

On the river, the board is Ts-Jd-6s-7c-5h. I have a pair of sevens. Flash checked to me, which is a sign of either weakness or a potential trap. My hand, a pair of sevens, is marginal and unlikely to get value from worse hands, while calling a raise would be difficult if I were to bet and get check-raised. Checking behind is the most prudent decision to see a showdown without risking further chips against potential straights or better pairs that Flash might have in their 3-betting range.

T8o is a weak offsuit two-gapper. From the button, unless we are trying to steal the blinds with a wider range, this is generally a fold in a 3-handed game against opponents who have shown a tendency to 3-bet and defend their blinds. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair of 3s. The button (Pro) has folded. The pot currently has 150 chips, and it's 50 more for me to call or I can raise. Pocket pairs, even low ones like 33, are strong starting hands in three-handed play, especially when folded to the blinds. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or to set up a post-flop play where I have the initiative.

Ace-Four offsuit is a defendable hand against a small blind raise in this position. The pot odds are favorable to call, and since I will have position post-flop against Flash, I can see a flop and evaluate based on the texture.

I have pocket threes on a 4-9-9 board. While I only have an underpair, this is a very dry board that likely missed most of Flashlight's calling range from the big blind. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should place a continuation bet to maintain initiative and potentially take the pot down right now. If I get raised, I will have to evaluate, but a standard c-bet of 300 (half pot) is appropriate here to test Flashlight's hand.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a paired board (9-9-4). The pot offers 3:1 odds to call the 300 chip bet. While I might be behind a nine or a pocket pair, folding this early against a small blind aggressor would be too weak given the pot odds. A call is appropriate here to see the turn and evaluate further, keeping the pot manageable.

The turn is the Jack of Spades, creating a board of 4s-9d-9h-Js. My pocket 3s are now a very weak hand, as any Jack or any 4 (though less likely) now beats me, and Flashlight's calling range on the flop (which was 4s-9d-9h) frequently includes 9s, pairs, or overcards that have now improved or still beat me. Since the pot is 1200 and Flashlight called my flop c-bet, they likely have some piece of the board or a draw (like 10-J or spades). I will check to see how Flashlight reacts; if they bet, I will likely have to fold given the strength of their range against my underpair.

With bottom pair on a paired board, I am essentially at the bottom of my range. Since the opponent checked, taking a free card to the river is the optimal play to maintain pot control and realize my showdown value without bloating the pot unnecessarily. A bet here would likely only be called by better hands.

On the river, the board is 4s-9d-9h-Js-Qd. My pocket 3s are now just an underpair and have very little showdown value. However, the way the hand has played out (Flashlight calling the flop and both of us checking the turn), it is unlikely a large bet will force a fold from any hand that currently beats me (like a 4, a J, or a Q). If Flashlight has Ace-high, they might check behind, and I might win, but if I bet, they likely fold their air and call with anything better. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play to reach a showdown as cheaply as possible. If Flashlight bets, I will fold as my hand is too weak to catch a bluff here.

I have a pair of fours on a board of 4-9-9-J-Q. My hand has very little showdown value against a range that includes any Jack, Queen, or Nine, all of which are highly probable in the opponent's betting range. Betting would only isolate myself against better hands that would call, while checking behind allows me to showdown my weak pair and avoid turning my hand into a bluff. Checking is the standard play here to minimize losses and realize whatever showdown value I have.

I have 9s8s on the button, which is a strong speculative hand. It plays well in multi-way pots and is a great candidate for a raise from this position to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. A standard 3x raise is 300.

I am in the small blind with 65 suited, which is a playable hand for speculative value. While I am out of position against the button's open raise, the pot odds are reasonable, and these cards have strong post-flop potential for draws or disguised sets/two-pairs. Calling is the standard approach to see a flop in this situation.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Three offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, calling here would be a long-term losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have bottom two pair with 6d5d on a Kd-6s-5s board. This is a very strong hand, but it is vulnerable to flush draws and straight draws. Since I am first to act on the flop, I should bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws, forcing the button to pay to see the next card if they have overcards or a draw.

I have a very strong semi-bluffing hand with a flush draw (9 spades remaining) and a gutshot straight draw (three 7s remaining, excluding the 7 of spades which is counted in the flush outs). With approximately 12 outs, I have significant equity (roughly 24% for the next card and nearly 48% to hit by the river). Flashlight's lead (donk bet) of 350 into a 700 pot suggests a King, a draw, or a mid-pair. By raising here, I take back the initiative, apply pressure to any of his marginal hands or draws, and potentially win the pot immediately. If called, I have position and massive equity to improve on the turn or river.

I hold bottom two pair (6d5d) on a board of Kd-6s-5s. This is a very strong hand, though vulnerable to flush draws (like two spades) and straight draws. Flash raised me to 1000 after my initial bet of 350. Folding is not an option as I am currently ahead of top pair hands like AK or KQ, which Flash likely has, and semi-bluffs. Raising would only isolate me against sets (KK, 66, 55) or better two-pair hands (K6, K5). Calling is the correct play to keep the pot manageable, keep his bluffs in, and re-evaluate the turn card.

The turn 2s is a dangerous card that completes potential flush draws on the board. My two-pair (6-5) is now significantly weakened and vulnerable to any spade in the opponent's range, as well as potential straights or better two-pair combinations. Given that I am out of position and the pot is already substantial, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and re-evaluate the opponent's intentions before committing more chips.

On the turn, the 2 of spades completes my flush (9-high). Flashlight's check after calling my flop raise indicates he likely has a King or a marginal hand that is now wary of the completed flush. I need to bet here for value to get paid by those hands (Kx, sets, or lower flushes). A bet of 1,350 (half pot) is a standard value sizing that keeps his calling range wide while building the pot in case I have the best hand.

The board has become extremely dangerous with the turn 2s completing a potential flush draw. My two-pair (6-5) is now essentially a bluff-catcher. Given that Flash raised on the flop and is betting into me on the turn after the flush card landed, the range of hands beating me (any spade, any set, any straight) is wide. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my remaining stack for a more favorable situation rather than paying off a potential flush.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong holding in a three-handed game, particularly on the button. I have the advantage of being in position, and raising allows me to seize initiative to either win the pot immediately or control the action in future streets. A raise to 300 is a standard opening size.

ATo is too strong to fold to a Button open in a 3-handed game, but calling out of position invites the Big Blind to squeeze and often leads to difficult post-flop spots. 3-betting to 900 takes the initiative, applies pressure to the Button's wide opening range, and gives us a chance to win the pot immediately.

I am in the big blind with 7d3d. Flashlight (Button) raised to 300, and Pro (Small Blind) 3-bet to 900. This is a very weak hand to defend against a 3-bet from the Small Blind, especially with the original raiser still to act. My hand is suited but lacks high-card value or strong connectivity, and I am out of position. The correct move is to fold.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong hand, especially in a three-handed game on the button. Since I have position and the hand is too strong to fold to a single 3-bet, calling is the most balanced strategic decision. I want to see the flop with initiative and avoid overcommitting to a 4-bet bluff that could lead to a difficult situation if I get jammed on.

This highly coordinated board (7-8-5) connects much better with my opponent's pre-flop calling range (which includes many suited connectors, mid-pairs, and sets) than with my 3-betting range (mostly big cards and high pairs). With only Ace-high, no strong draws, and being out of position, continuing with a bet is very low expected-value. Checking is the most prudent play to evaluate the opponent's action, and I am prepared to fold to any significant aggression.

The board texture [7s, 8d, 5h] is highly coordinated and interacts well with a typical defending range that includes many pairs, straight draws, and connectors. My holding of Ace-Jack offsuit is essentially a total air hand here with no pair, no draw, and only two overcards. Since the opponent (Pro) checked to me, betting as a bluff would be risky as they are likely to call with any piece of this board. Checking behind is the most prudent play to maintain control over the pot size, preserve my stack, and see a free turn card where I might improve.

The turn card, the 4 of clubs, makes the board even more coordinated (7-8-5-4), meaning any 6 now has a straight. This board texture overwhelmingly favors my opponent's pre-flop calling range, which contains many medium pairs, suited connectors, and one-gappers. My Ace-high has virtually zero equity and I hold no draws. Bluffing into this board is reckless as I have no fold equity against any pair or draw, so the best action is to check and prepare to fold to a bet.

The board is extremely connected ([7s, 8d, 5h, 4c]), making it dangerous for my Ace-High hand. Pro has checked twice, which could indicate a trap with a made hand or simply a weak holding. Betting into this board is unlikely to force a fold from any pair or better, and I am not looking to bloat the pot with high-card strength. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize any remaining equity, hoping to either see a cheap showdown or hit an Ace on the river to improve to top pair.

The river places a 4-5-6-7-8 straight on the board. My hole cards (Tc Ah) do not improve this, meaning I am strictly playing the board for an 8-high straight. Any 9 in my opponent's range beats me with a higher straight (9-high). Since my opponent called a pre-flop 3-bet, hands like 99, T9s, J9s, or 98s are perfectly reasonable for them to have. Betting here serves no purpose because I cannot get called by a worse hand, and I would only get action from a 9 that beats me. Therefore, the optimal play is to check, expecting to chop the pot against most of their range, and to re-evaluate if facing a bet.

The board shows a straight (4-5-6-7-8), which means every player in the hand effectively has this straight. My hand (Ace-high) provides no additional value, and I do not have a 9 to form a higher straight. Betting into this board is pointless as it will likely only be called by a hand that beats me (a 9), or it will lead to a chop. Checking behind is the optimal play to secure my share of the pot and avoid any unnecessary risk.

Ten-Eight offsuit is a marginal-to-weak hand. While we are on the Button and could attempt a steal, folding is the standard and safest play to conserve chips for better starting hands, especially against two opponents with deep stacks.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 4h. The Button (Pro) has folded. The pot currently consists of my 50 small blind and Flashlight's 100 big blind.
