K6o is a weak starting hand, even on the button in a 3-handed game. There's no need to force a steal here and play a marginal hand post-flop, especially against opponents who have shown a willingness to 3-bet or call wide. Folding preserves my stack for more profitable opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Six suited (Kc 6c). After the Button folds, the action is on me. Kc 6c is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the Big Blind, who has shown a tendency to fold to raises in previous hands.

Deuce-five offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With no connectivity and no high-card strength, playing this hand out of position against an aggressive raiser like Flash is a negative expected value decision. The pot odds of 2:1 are insufficient to warrant calling with such a marginal holding, and it is better to fold and conserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with K7o. This is a standard hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and pick up the pot. My stack is very healthy at 14,075. Raising to 300 has been my consistent sizing throughout the tournament for opening pots. If either blind defends, I have position for the rest of the hand.

K-4 offsuit is a weak holding, particularly out of position in the small blind against a button open-raise. Playing this hand would likely result in a negative expected value situation. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Jack-Ten offsuit is a marginal hand, especially out of position facing a 3x raise from the chip leader on the Button. The pot odds are okay, but playing JTo out of position often leads to dominated situations or tricky post-flop decisions with reverse implied odds. Folding is the most standard and solid play to preserve stack.

With 6s 8c in the button position, this is a very weak, unsuited hand with no connectors or potential. Playing this hand against two opponents provides poor expected value. It is standard to fold here to conserve my stack.
97o is too weak of a hand to play from the small blind out of position, even for a steal attempt or a limp against the big blind. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve chips.

54s is a solid suited connector. Opening from the button gives us the opportunity to either steal the blinds immediately or play a pot in position with a hand that can flop very well. Our standard open size has been 3 big blinds.

I have been dealt a pocket pair of Sixes in the Small Blind. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. With a stack of over 14,000 chips, I am the chip leader. Pocket sixes are a strong hand three-handed, but playing them out of position against an aggressive raise requires caution. However, folding here would be too tight. I will call to see a flop, keeping the pot manageable while retaining the set-mining potential and the possibility that my pair is currently the best hand.

The pot size is 700, and it only costs 200 to call, offering excellent pot odds of 3.5:1. Suited connectors like JcTc perform well in multi-way pots, and acting last post-flop is a significant positional advantage. Calling is the most profitable play here to see a flop cheaply.

I have flopped a set of sixes on a board of Th-6h-Kc. This is a monster hand, but the board is relatively 'wet' with a flush draw and some straight possibilities (QJ, J9). Since Pro was the pre-flop aggressor, I will check to him to see if he continues with a continuation bet, which would allow me to check-raise and build the pot immediately. In a three-way pot, there is a good chance someone has a piece of this board (like a King or a heart draw) and will be willing to commit chips.

I have middle pair with a Ten on a King-high board. This is a vulnerable hand against the pre-flop raiser, who likely has a strong range including Kings. Checking allows me to control the pot and re-evaluate on the turn.

With a 9-out flush draw on a K-T-6 board and both opponents checking to me, this is an excellent spot to continuation bet as a semi-bluff. A bet of around half the pot will fold out their weak hands and random overcards while building the pot in case I hit my flush on the turn or river.

I have flopped a set of sixes, which is an extremely strong hand on this board. Pro has followed up their pre-flop aggression with a half-pot continuation bet. While calling could potentially trap Flashlight or keep Pro's bluffs in, the board is coordinated with heart draws and straight possibilities. I want to build the pot now and get value from hands like top pair (Kx) or strong draws. A check-raise to 1350 puts pressure on the opponents and protects my hand against various draws while building a pot that I am currently a huge favorite to win.

I have middle pair with a gutshot straight draw on a board that contains a King. The betting action (a bet followed by a raise) indicates significant strength from my opponents, likely a made hand like a King, two-pair, or a set. My pair of Tens is extremely vulnerable and likely behind both opponents' ranges. Given the aggressive action and the strength of the board, I do not have the pot odds or the hand strength to continue.

Facing a check-raise from Flash on the flop, we have a 9-out flush draw with 5h 4h. We need to call 900 to win a total pot of 3600, meaning we need exactly 25% equity to break even on a direct pot odds basis. With two cards to come, our flush draw gives us roughly 35% equity against most value ranges (like top pair or two pair). Calling is the standard and most profitable decision, as it allows us to easily realize our equity and leverage our positional advantage on the turn and river without overcommitting with a low flush draw.

The turn 3d is a complete brick that does not change the board texture. Having flopped a set of sixes, I am still extremely likely to have the best hand. Pro called my flop check-raise, which indicates a range consisting of strong Kings (AK, KQ, KJ), flush draws, or perhaps an open-ended straight draw (QJ). By betting 1800, which is half the pot, I am continuing to build the pot for value against his made hands while charging his draws to see the river. This size is also designed to keep his range wide enough to call.

Facing a 1800 bet on the turn, the pot is 5400 and we need to call 1800, giving us 3:1 pot odds (or needing exactly 25% equity to break even). The 3d on the turn improved our hand significantly; not only do we have a flush draw with our 5h 4h on a board with two hearts, but we also picked up an open-ended straight draw (any 2 or 7). This gives us 9 flush outs and 6 additional non-heart straight outs, totaling 15 clean outs. With one card to come, 15 outs provide us with roughly 32.6% direct equity against most of Flash's made hands. Since our equity exceeds the required pot odds, calling is strictly profitable. Additionally, we have implied odds if we hit, as Flash's strong line (flop check-raise, turn lead) indicates a hand he is unlikely to fold on the river. Raising here risks facing a 3-bet shove that could deny us our equity, making a flat call in position the optimal play.

On the river, the board is Th-6h-Kc-3d-Jh. I have a set of sixes, which is a very strong hand. However, the river card (Jack of Hearts) is quite coordinated. It completes a possible flush (any two hearts), a straight (AQ), and also improves hands like KJ to two pair or JJ to a higher set. Given that I have been the aggressor on the flop and turn, checking here is a prudent move. It allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially induce a bluff from missed straight draws (like QJ if they didn't have hearts) or see a cheaper showdown against a King-high hand. If Pro bets, I will have to evaluate based on his sizing, but with a set, I am likely calling most reasonable bets despite the dangerous board texture.

The Jh on the river completes our flush. While it is only a 5-high flush, Flash's line of check-raising the flop, betting the turn, and then checking the river when the flush hits strongly indicates a made hand like a set (TT, 66) or two pair (KT) that is now afraid of the flush. A higher flush would likely continue betting for value. Therefore, checking behind misses value, and shoving risks getting called only by better flushes. Betting a smaller amount, roughly one-third of the pot (around 2500), is the optimal sizing to extract crying calls from his sets and two pairs, while allowing us to safely fold if he happens to check-raise all-in with a slow-played higher flush.

I have a set of sixes, which is an extremely strong hand. The river Jack of Hearts is a concerning card as it completes several draws: any two hearts make a flush, AQ makes a straight, and KJ improves to two pair. However, my set remains very high in my range. Pro has bet 2,500 into a 7,200 pot, giving me pot odds of nearly 3.9-to-1. At these odds, I only need to be right about 20% of the time to justify a call. Pro could be value betting a top-pair hand like AK or KQ, or potentially bluffing with a missed straight draw that did not include hearts. Given the strength of my hand and the favorable odds, folding is not an option, but raising is also risky as it would likely only be called by hands that have me beaten. I will call to see the showdown.

I am on the button with T7o. The pot is small, and I am the first to act pre-flop (excluding the blinds). While T7o is not a strong hand, it is occasionally playable for a steal or a min-raise in a three-handed game to put pressure on the blinds. However, given the tournament context and my current stack of 8325, there is little incentive to play a marginal hand out of position post-flop if called. The most disciplined play here is to fold and wait for a better opening or a more favorable situation.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak hand, Queen-Deuce offsuit. Given I am out of position against a strong and deep-stacked opponent (Pro in the Big Blind), there is no value in playing this hand. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Four offsuit. This is a standard hand to open for a raise in a three-handed game to steal the blinds or take initiative into the flop. I will raise to 300 to pressure the blinds.

KTo is a marginal hand to play from the Small Blind out of position against a Button raise. It suffers from reverse implied odds and we are not closing the action, meaning the Big Blind could still squeeze. Folding is the safest and standard play here to preserve the chip lead.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 4s. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. This is a standard opening size. J4o is a weak hand and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against Flashlight who has shown aggression in previous hands. Given the current stack sizes and the hand's equity, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.
I am on the Button with an extremely weak hand (9h 2s). The optimal strategy is to fold this pre-flop and wait for better starting hands.

With the Button folding, I am in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. Queen-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in this spot. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and exert pressure on Flashlight, who has a shorter stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit, which is a strong enough hand to defend against an opening raise from the Small Blind. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a pot of 400), and I need to defend my blind against potential aggression from Flash.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a heads-up pot against the Big Blind. The flop comes monotone with three hearts (Ah 8h 2h). I hold the Queen of Hearts, which gives me a very strong flush draw (the second nut draw behind the King of Hearts, as the Ace is on the board). Betting here is the standard play for a pre-flop raiser on an Ace-high board to maintain initiative. A half-pot bet (300) allows me to take down the pot immediately if Flashlight doesn't have an Ace or a heart, while also building a pot for when I potentially hit my flush.

I hold top pair with an Ace, which is generally a strong hand, but the board texture is extremely dangerous due to the potential flush draw (three hearts). However, holding the Ace of Diamonds (Ad) provides me with a blocker to the nut flush, making my hand slightly more resilient. Given the pot odds of 300 into a 900 pot (1:3 or 25%), calling is mathematically sound. Raising is too aggressive given the board's volatility and my weak kicker, so I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate.

I have the second nut flush draw with the Queen of Hearts. After my flop bet was called, the turn is a blank (6d). Checking here is a prudent play to control the pot size and realize my equity. If Flashlight checks back, I get a free card to hit my flush. If he bets, I can likely call a reasonable sizing given my high-equity draw and the pot odds.

I have two pair (Aces and Sixes) on a wet board with three hearts. While the flush draw is a concern, I have the Ace of Diamonds as a blocker. Since my opponent checked to me, I should bet for value to extract chips from weaker Aces, pairs, or draws that might call a standard bet. Betting allows me to define my hand's strength and charge any flush draws that are currently chasing.

I am holding the Queen of Hearts, giving me the second-nut flush draw on a monotone board. Flashlight has bet 600 into a 1200 pot, providing me with 3:1 pot odds (requiring 25% equity to break even). While my direct odds of hitting the flush on the river are approximately 19.5%, the implied odds make this a clear call. Flashlight has about 3925 chips remaining behind; if I hit my flush on the river, I can likely extract significant value or even play for his entire stack if he holds a lower flush or a strong Ace. Folding is too conservative given the equity of a high flush draw, and raising might force out bluffs while getting called by better hands, so calling is the most profitable play.

I missed my flush draw and currently hold only Queen-high. The board has paired with an eight on the river, making the situation more dangerous. Given that Flashlight called the flop and bet the turn, he likely holds a made hand like an Ace, a flush, or now potentially trips with the eight. While my Queen of Hearts acts as a blocker to some flushes, it is unlikely a bluff will succeed against an opponent who has shown consistent interest in the pot. I will check and hope for a cheap showdown, though I expect to fold if faced with a significant bet.

I hold two pair (Aces and Eights) on a board of Ah-8h-2h-6d-8s. The river paired the 8, which is a dangerous card as it completes any hand with an 8. Since Flash checked, checking behind is the optimal play to realize my showdown value. Betting would be risky as it would likely only be called by better hands (like an 8 or a flush) while folding out bluffs or weaker pairs that I currently beat. Checking allows me to reach showdown safely.

In a 3-handed game, being on the button allows for a wider opening range to put pressure on the blinds and take advantage of position. J5s is a marginal hand but carries some playability due to being suited. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to attempt to win the blinds or play a pot in position.

My starting hand (3d, 6h) is extremely weak, disconnected, and offsuit. It has very poor playability, especially playing out of position in the small blind against a button open. With the Big Blind still to act and my stack needing protection, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my chips for a more favorable situation.

Queen-Ten offsuit is a marginal hand that often plays poorly out of position. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, the best decision is to fold rather than call and get involved in a tricky post-flop situation where reverse implied odds can be a problem.

Jack-Ten offsuit is a playable starting hand in the Button position. I want to utilize my positional advantage to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds. A raise to 300 is a standard opening size, allowing me to build the pot and evaluate post-flop play based on the board texture.

A6o is a marginal offsuit Ace, and playing it out of position from the Small Blind against a 3x Button open is unprofitable. Calling often leads to domination scenarios, and there are better hands to 3-bet. Folding is the standard and safest play.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7c-3h, which is an extremely weak hand. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the button, and Pro has folded from the Small Blind. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call, 7c-3h plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. I will fold to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

AQo is a premium starting hand, particularly on the button in a 3-handed game. I want to build the pot and take control of the hand pre-flop. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard and effective sizing.

I am in the Small Blind with K3o. The Button (Pro), who is the chip leader, has raised to 3BB. While I have a King, my kicker is extremely weak, and I will be playing the rest of the hand out of position against a skilled, aggressive player. In a three-handed game, K3o is marginal, but against a raise from the chip leader who can easily put pressure on my remaining stack, it is safer to fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips. My goal is to maximize my stack over the 150 hands, and calling here likely leads to a negative expected value situation post-flop.

Holding 6h-9s, which is a weak, unsuited hand, I have poor connectivity and equity to call a raise from the button. Even though the price is relatively cheap to call, defending the big blind with such a marginal hand OOP is generally negative EV. It is better to fold, conserve the stack, and wait for a more favorable spot to play.

I am on the button with 6h 5h, a strong speculative hand (suited connectors). The pot is currently 150 with a small 50/100 blind structure. I want to enter the pot to realize my equity and potentially outplay my opponents post-flop, as suited connectors perform well in multi-way or heads-up pots when played with position. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate to take control of the action.

My starting hand (4h, 2s) is extremely weak and offers very poor equity, especially against an open raise from the button. Calling 250 chips into a 450 chip pot while being forced to play the remainder of the hand out of position is negative expected value. Given the stack depths and the strength of the hand, the most disciplined and strategic decision is to fold and preserve my chip stack for a more favorable situation.

Facing a standard button raise with a weak, unsuited hand like Q8o from the Big Blind, folding is the most fundamentally sound and profitable play over the long run. Q8o struggles heavily in terms of playability and equity realization out of position.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack suited, which is a very strong starting hand. Given the stack depths and my position, raising is the standard play to build the pot and leverage my positional advantage over the blinds.

AJo is a strong hand against a button open. As the Small Blind, 3-betting is preferred to calling to take the betting lead, define ranges, and discourage the Big Blind from squeezing or overcalling. A 3-bet to 900 is a standard sizing to accomplish these goals.

I am in the Big Blind holding King of Hearts and Five of Hearts. Flashlight opened from the Button, and Pro 3-bet from the Small Blind. Facing a 3-bet from the chip leader while being out of position with a marginal hand like K5s is a losing proposition in the long run. I should preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I opened the pot with Ace-Jack suited, and the Small Blind (Pro) 3-bet. AJs is a strong hand that plays well post-flop, and calling is mathematically and strategically sound given the stack depths and my position. 4-betting here could commit too many chips too early against a potentially strong range, so I will call to see a flop and evaluate based on the texture.

I completely missed the flop of 4s Qs 8h with my Ace-Jack offsuit. My opponent called a 3-bet pre-flop, meaning their range is relatively strong and contains plenty of hands that connect with this board, such as AQ, KQ, QJs, middle pocket pairs, and spade flush draws. A continuation bet here as a bluff has low success equity given how well the board hits their perceived range. Checking is the most conservative and standard play here to see how the opponent reacts.

I have the nut flush draw on a board of 4s-Qs-8h. Pro checked to me. Since I hold the nut flush draw, I have significant equity even if Pro has a made hand like top pair. Betting allows me to build the pot if I hit and provides fold equity against some of Pro's range. A bet of 700 is appropriate relative to the 1900 pot, putting pressure on their holdings while maintaining a manageable stack-to-pot ratio.

I completely missed the flop and have only Ace-high with no draws. After I checked, my opponent bet, indicating they likely connected with the board (e.g., a Queen, a set, or a strong flush draw) or are at least testing my check. Without any substantial equity or a reason to turn this hand into a bluff, folding is the clear and standard play.
T7 offsuit is a weak hand. Even though I am on the button and could attempt a steal, I have a comfortable chip lead and do not need to play junk hands. Folding is the standard and safest play.

With the action folded to me in the small blind, I am holding Queen-Six suited. In a three-handed game, this is a strong enough hand to open for a raise against the big blind to try and take down the pot immediately or play post-flop with initiative. My stack is healthy, and I have successfully used a 300-chip opening size in previous hands to win blinds.

Holding Queen-Deuce offsuit in the Big Blind against a raise from the Small Blind is a clear fold. The hand has poor equity, and being out of position makes it difficult to play post-flop. I should conserve my chips for a stronger hand.

I am on the button with 8s 6h. While I have enough chips to attempt a steal, 86o is a relatively weak hand to open with, especially since Pro (the big blind) has been active and has a massive chip lead. My strategy here is to stay disciplined and wait for better spots to recover from my loss in hand #100, rather than risking chips on a low-equity bluffing opportunity.

I am in the small blind with 4c 5c after the button folded. With suited connectors, I have decent playability and can comfortably call to see a flop heads-up against the Big Blind. Raising is an option, but completing is a solid, lower-variance play to see if I can hit the flop hard.

My hand is T2o, which is a very weak hand. Since the Small Blind just limped, I can check my option in the Big Blind to see a free flop. Raising with such a weak hand out of position is unnecessary and risky, and folding is not required since I already have my blind posted and no extra chips to call.

I have bottom pair (fours) with a gutshot straight draw on a board of 4-3-J. As the small blind, betting into the big blind with such a marginal hand is suboptimal. Checking is the standard play to keep the pot controlled and see the turn cheaply, allowing me to evaluate further based on the opponent's action and the next card.
With Ten-high and no draw on a dry board, I have very little equity. Since Flashlight checked, I can just check behind to see a free turn card. There's no compelling reason to bluff at this stage.

The turn card, the 7 of clubs, gives me a flush draw to go with my pair of 4s and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6 for a 3-4-5-6-7 straight). Since my opponent checked the flop, their range appears relatively weak or capped. By betting here, I am semi-bluffing to capitalize on my equity and potentially take down the pot immediately. If called, I have plenty of outs to improve to the best hand on the river. A bet of 100 into the 200 pot is a balanced sizing to exert pressure.

I have Ten-high with no pair and no draw on a 4s-3d-Jc-7c board. Flashlight has bet on the turn, and I have absolutely no equity or reason to invest any chips into this pot. Folding is the only viable option.

Queen-deuce offsuit is a very weak hand with low board coverage and poor playability. As the button, I prefer to fold and wait for a stronger opening hand to conserve my stack rather than risking chips with this unsuited, disconnected pair.

With A2o in the small blind and the button folding, this is a prime opportunity to steal the big blind. Raising to 3 big blinds puts pressure on the big blind to fold weak hands and gives me the betting lead going to the flop if called.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-4 offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I am getting 2-to-1 odds to call, 7-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against the chip leader. Given the tournament stage, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
A9s is a strong hand from the Button. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a strong suited Ace.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Seven offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard 3x raise. K7o is a marginal hand to play out of position against an aggressive chip leader. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff with this hand isn't ideal given the stack sizes and the likelihood of being called. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Holding 2d 6c, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. With no suited connection or high card value, calling a raise from the button is highly negative EV. I should conserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Q2 offsuit. While it is a marginal hand, in a three-handed game, the button should be opening a wide range to steal the blinds. However, I have seen 'Flashlight' and 'Pro' play aggressively in previous hands. Given the current stack distributions and my position, a standard raise here would be around 300. However, Q2o is quite weak and often dominated if called. Looking at the table dynamics, I will fold this hand to maintain my stack for a better spot, as I have enough chips to wait for a stronger opening hand.

King-Jack offsuit is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario, especially with the button having already folded. Raising to 300 is a standard move to seize the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or establish a commanding position against the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 64o, a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. The Small Blind raised to 300 (3x the big blind). Against this raise, defending with such a trash hand is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my large stack.

I am in the button position with 7-2 offsuit, which is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Given the poor quality of the cards and the fact that I am not in a position to exploit the blinds effectively with this hand, the most disciplined and mathematically correct play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.
I have Ace-King offsuit in the Small Blind after the Button folded. This is a premium starting hand. I will raise to a standard 300 chips to build the pot for value and put pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack suited, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game, especially in a blind-versus-blind situation. The Small Blind (Pro) has opened for 300. Given my hand strength and the fact that I will have position throughout the hand, I will 3-bet to 900 to build the pot and take initiative. This amount is large enough to put pressure on his opening range while allowing me to easily call a 4-bet or play post-flop if called.

With AK offsuit facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind, a 4-bet is the optimal play to extract value, deny equity, and put pressure on Flash's ~70 BB stack. By 4-betting to 2400, I define my range as very strong and force him to make a tough decision, potentially committing a large portion of his stack pre-flop.

I have Ace-Jack suited in the Big Blind, which is a very strong hand in 3-handed play. After I 3-bet to 900, Pro (the Small Blind) responded with a 4-bet to 2,400. While a 4-bet often represents a strong range, Pro has shown significant aggression and a willingness to play creatively in previous hands (like Hand #32 and Hand #60). Given the pot odds (calling 1,500 to win a total pot of 4,800) and the hand's equity against a standard 4-bet range in a short-handed game, folding is too conservative. Shoving might only get called by better hands, so I will call the 4-bet to see a flop in position. This will leave me with an SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) of approximately 0.77, meaning I am essentially committed to the hand on most favorable or neutral textures.

We flopped Top Pair, Top Kicker on a draw-heavy board (Qc 5c Ks). Given the pre-flop 4-bet action, the pot is 4800 and the effective stack is only 4625, leaving us with an SPR of less than 1. Betting around 1/3 of the pot (1500 chips) is ideal to extract value from weaker hands like AQ, KQ, or flush draws, and might induce our opponent to bluff-shove their remaining stack. We are fully committed to getting all the chips in.

On a board of Kc-Qc-5c, my Ace-Jack of hearts is in a precarious position against a range that 4-bet pre-flop. The Small Blind's 4-bet range is heavily weighted towards premium pairs (AA, KK, QQ) and strong kings like AK, all of which have me either drawing to only four outs (the Tens) or virtually dead. Although the bet of 1,500 into a 4,800 pot offers 4.2:1 pot odds, my equity is insufficient to justify a call. A gutshot straight draw provides about an 8.5% chance of hitting on the turn, which falls short of the roughly 24% equity needed. Furthermore, hitting an Ace might not be safe, and a fourth club could ruin a straight. Preserving my remaining 4,625 chips (46 big blinds) for a more advantageous situation is the superior long-term strategy.
KJo is a solid starting hand on the Button in a 3-handed game. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate to try to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a strong broadway hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit (Q2o). The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. Q2o is a very weak hand, and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand if I call. Pro has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively. There is no reason to defend such a weak hand out of position; folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play here to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the big blind with a pocket pair of sixes. Getting 200 to call into a pot of 450 is a clear call, especially heads-up against a button raise. Playing out of position, I want to keep the pot manageable while looking to hit a set or a favorable low board. Raising in this spot can be volatile, so a flat call is the standard and most profitable decision given the pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of sixes on a flop of 9h-5s-Qs. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Pro), the standard play is to check to the aggressor. My hand has some showdown value, but it is currently vulnerable to any Queen, Nine, or higher card. Checking allows me to evaluate Pro's action and keep the pot controlled, as I have no clear reason to bet into the button range on this board.

I have King-Jack offsuit with a gutshot straight draw to the nuts on a Queen-high wet board. Since the board connects well with the Big Blind's defending range, and there are flush draws present, checking back is the safest play. It allows me to realize my equity for free, control the pot size, and avoid getting check-raised out of the hand.

I am holding a pair of sixes on a board of 9h-5s-Qs-Kh. Given that both the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and I have checked through the flop and the turn, it is highly likely that neither of us has connected strongly with this board, or we are both playing cautiously. My pocket sixes are currently a low-strength hand that is effectively a bluff-catcher. There is no incentive to bet here as I would only be called by better hands, and checking behind allows me to keep the pot small and see the river for free.

On the turn, I hit top pair with a King and have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts. The board is very wet with spade and heart flush draws, as well as straight draws. Since the Big Blind checked again, I should bet to charge draws and get value from weaker pairs like a Queen or a Nine. A bet of roughly half the pot protects my equity and builds the pot.

I am facing a bet of 350 into a pot of 600 on a turn board of 9h-5s-Qs-Kh. My pocket sixes are extremely weak here, having no draw and no showdown value against a range that would bet on this King-high board. Pro's turn bet after checking the flop suggests they may have hit the King or are betting a Queen, both of which have me crushed. There is no mathematical justification for calling with 6-high when I am effectively drawing dead, so I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jh Th, a strong suited connector that plays very well in position. Standard play here is to open with a raise to 3x the big blind to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds.

I am in the small blind with 7c-9s, a weak offsuit hand. Playing this out of position against a button open is not strategically sound. Given the current tournament state and the strength of the hand, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

A5o is a marginal hand, especially when out of position against a button raise. While 3-betting as a bluff is an option, calling with a weak offsuit Ace often leads to difficult post-flop spots. Given our commanding chip lead, there's no need to take unnecessary risks with a easily dominated hand. Folding is the safest and most solid play.

I hold a premium pocket pair, Jacks (Jh, Jc), on the button. This is a strong hand that plays well in position. A raise is standard here to build the pot, isolate the blinds, and gain initiative. A raise to 300 is consistent with my typical opening size and allows me to see the flop with control.

3d 5h is an extremely weak, disconnected, offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the button, playing this hand out of position in the small blind would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 3c, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the button, and the Small Blind has folded. To call, I would need to put in 200 more chips into a pot that would then be 650. Given the poor quality of my hand and being out of position for the rest of the hand against Flashlight, a fold is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

With A6o on the button in a 3-handed game, I have a decent ace that plays well enough to steal the blinds or play in position. I'll open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to put pressure on the shorter stacked blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300, which is a standard opening size. Pro is the chip leader and has been active, so their range is likely wide. A7o is strong enough to defend, especially since I'm getting decent pot odds (250 to call into a pot of 450). While 3-betting is an option to isolate or take the pot down, calling allows me to see a flop without overcommitting my medium stack against the tournament leader. I'll call and evaluate on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with suited connectors (7s 5s). There is a raise from the button and a call from the small blind. The pot is currently 700 chips, and I only need to call 200 chips to see the flop, giving me 3.5:1 pot odds. Suited connectors play well in multi-way pots, and the price is right to continue.

The flop is paired with threes (3h-Tc-3s), which is a board that likely missed everyone's range. I have Ace-high. In a three-way pot, leading out here is generally not advisable without a strong hand or a clear draw. Checking is the most standard and safest play to see how the Button (the pre-flop aggressor) and the Big Blind react. I will evaluate the situation further if there is a bet.

The flop is dry with a paired 3, offering minimal protection or improvement for my 7-high hand. I have no showdown value and no equity to speak of, so checking is the only logical move. I will look to fold if there is any aggression behind me.

The flop comes paired and relatively dry (3h Tc 3s). Both opponents checked to me, which usually indicates weakness or a marginal made hand. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet here is highly profitable since it puts pressure on random overcards and smaller pocket pairs. A 1/3 pot sizing (300 chips) is sufficient to take down the pot against air while keeping my risk low in a 3-way pot.

I have Ace-high on a dry, paired board (3h-Tc-3s). The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has placed a small continuation bet of 1/3 pot. This board likely missed most of his range, and Ace-high often holds enough equity to continue, especially given the 4:1 pot odds. Raising here is unnecessary as it would only fold out weaker hands and get called by better ones. Calling is the most prudent move to see a turn and evaluate the action from the player behind me (Flashlight).

The board is paired (3h 3s) and I hold 7s 5s, which is complete air with no equity. With two opponents already in the pot—one betting and one calling—my 7-high hand is effectively worthless. There is no reason to put more chips into the pot, as I am behind any pair, Ace-high, or King-high, which are all well within their ranges. Folding is the only disciplined, professional decision.

On the turn, the board has paired with threes and is relatively dry, although a spade draw is now possible. I have Ace-high, which still has some showdown value on this specific texture but is not strong enough to lead out into the pre-flop aggressor. Checking to the Button (Pro) is the most standard and disciplined play here. This allows me to control the pot size and see how Pro reacts to the turn. I will evaluate whether to call or fold if he decides to place another bet.

With Ace-high and no strong draw on a 3-T-3-4 board, I don't have much value to bet. Flash called my flop continuation bet, which means he likely has a piece of this board, such as a Ten, a pocket pair like 55-99, or a floating draw that just picked up equity. A turn bet would bloat the pot against a short-stacked opponent who might check-shove, forcing me to fold. Checking behind provides pot control, allows me to realize my equity, and sets up a potential showdown against missed overcards or draws.

The board is 3h-Tc-3s-4s-8h. I have Ace-high (Ad 7h). The pre-flop aggressor, Pro, bet the flop but checked back on the turn, which often indicates either a marginal hand or a complete miss. My Ace-high has significant showdown value in this spot, as many of Pro's missed high-card hands (like QJ, KJ, etc.) are currently losing to me. Betting here doesn't achieve much, as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I don't need to bluff Pro off a better hand given the way the action has unfolded. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown.

On the river, the board is 3h-Tc-3s-4s-8h and Flash has checked to me for a third time. My Ace-high has some showdown value against missed straight or flush draws that he might have floated on the flop. A bet here is unlikely to get a better hand like a weak Ten or a medium pocket pair (55-99) to fold, and worse hands will certainly not call. Checking behind is the safest and most profitable play to realize whatever showdown equity I have without risking more chips in a marginally profitable spot.

I am on the button with 5d 2h, which is arguably one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While the button is a great position to steal the blinds, both Flashlight and Pro have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet in previous hands. Given the strength of my hand and my chip stack relative to the blinds, there is no reason to risk chips on a high-variance steal attempt. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I am in the Small Blind with suited connectors (Jd Td) after the button folds. Raising to 300 is a standard aggressive move to take control of the hand heads-up against the Big Blind and potentially win the pot pre-flop or build value with a strong drawing hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Q5o, a very weak hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, calling or 3-betting with this hand is not profitable in the long run. Since I have a massive chip lead, there's no need to unnecessarily bleed chips defending with trash hands. I will fold.

I am on the button with a weak hand (Jack-Seven offsuit). Given the stage of the tournament (Hand 120/150) and the weak strength of my starting hand, folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack. Stealing the blinds with this hand is too marginal against a table that has been fairly active.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-King offsuit, a premium starting hand. With the Button folding, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. I want to build the pot and take the betting initiative. A standard raise to 3 big blinds (300 chips) is optimal to extract value from worse hands or take down the blinds immediately without revealing the exact strength of my hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand, especially in a blind-versus-blind scenario. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Pro has shown to be quite active from the blinds in previous hands. While calling is an option to keep the pot controlled, 3-betting is the standard value play here to put pressure on the Small Blind's wider opening range and to take the initiative in the hand. I will raise to 1000, which is a standard 3-bet size (roughly 3.3x the open).

With Ace-King offsuit, I have one of the strongest starting hands in poker. Flash's 3-bet from the Big Blind to 1000 chips leaves him with 3775 behind (about 38 BBs). Given my positional disadvantage post-flop, the strength of my hand, and his relatively short stack, 4-betting all-in is the optimal play. It maximizes fold equity, denies him the opportunity to realize his equity with marginal hands, and guarantees we see all five cards if he calls with a pocket pair, often flipping or dominating his calling range.

I am in a blind-versus-blind battle with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I originally 3-bet to 1000, and the Small Blind (Pro) has responded by 4-betting me all-in. Pro is the chip leader and has been consistently aggressive. In this specific positional matchup (SB vs BB), ranges are at their widest. While Pro's 4-bet shows strength, AQo has sufficient equity against a typical aggressive 4-betting range to justify calling for my remaining stack. I need approximately 37% equity to call, and against a range of pocket pairs, AK, and other Broadway hands, AQo is well-positioned to double up here. Folding would leave me with less than 30 big blinds while the other two players have significantly more. I will commit my stack and call.

With A4o on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a standard open-raise to steal the blinds. Holding an Ace blocks some of our opponents' strongest possible hands, and taking the initiative gives us a good chance to pick up the pot right here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a very strong hand in three-handed play. The Button (Pro) has opened for 300. Pro has been playing aggressively, especially recently. While calling is an option, 3-betting allows me to take initiative, potentially end the hand immediately, or isolate the Button if the Big Blind folds. I will raise to 1,000 to put pressure on Pro's opening range and define the strength of my hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit, which is a weak hand to defend against a raise and a 3-bet. Being out of position against two aggressive opponents, the risk of investing more chips into a likely dominated situation is not worth it. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

After opening A4o from the button, we are faced with a 3-bet from the Small Blind. A4o is a marginal opening hand and does not play well against 3-bets, as it is often dominated and has poor playability post-flop. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit (J2o). While being on the button provides a positional advantage, J2o is one of the weakest hands in the range and generally doesn't play well post-flop, especially against a chip leader (Pro) who has been aggressive in recent hands. Given the current tournament stage and my healthy stack, there is no reason to risk chips on a bottom-tier hand. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

With the button folding, I am in a heads-up situation against the big blind. Queen-high suited is a strong enough hand to raise and attempt to steal the blinds in this position. Raising to 300 is a standard size to apply pressure and maximize my potential to take down the pot pre-flop.

K2 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a 3x open raise from the Small Blind, calling or 3-betting with this holding is largely unprofitable despite having position post-flop. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve chips for better opportunities.
I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a strong hand to open with in a three-handed game. Raising to 300 is a standard move to steal the blinds or take initiative in the hand.

In the Small Blind with A4s facing a Button open, I have a great candidate for a 3-bet bluff. A4 suited has good fold equity, an Ace blocker to the strongest hands in my opponent's range, and the potential to make the nut flush or straight if called. 3-betting to 1000 puts significant pressure on the Button's opening range and takes the initiative out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-5 offsuit. There has been a raise to 300 from Flashlight on the button and a 3-bet to 1000 from Pro in the Small Blind. My hand is very weak and does not play well in multi-way pots or against the strong ranges represented by a 3-bet from the SB. Folding is the only viable strategic move here to preserve my stack.

I opened to 300 from the button with Ace-Eight offsuit, a standard opening, but Pro has 3-bet to 1000. A8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly against a 3-betting range, which usually contains stronger Aces or pocket pairs. Given that I am out of position to Pro's 3-betting range and A8o is frequently dominated, the most disciplined play at this stage of the tournament is to fold and preserve my stack.
Holding 9s 6c on the Button, this is a very weak and unplayable starting hand. I will simply fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Five offsuit. Since the Button has folded, it is only between me and the Big Blind. While J5o is a hand I could occasionally use to steal the blinds, it is generally too weak to call or raise with from this position, especially since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand if the Big Blind calls. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 8h 7s. In a three-handed game, this is a standard hand to open-raise with from the button to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. I will use the standard opening size of 300 that has been consistent throughout the tournament.

K9o is a reasonable hand to defend against a Button open, particularly given the pot odds offered. Although I am out of position, my stack depth of 71 BB allows for a controlled approach. Calling 300 is a standard move to see a flop, balancing defensive range requirements while avoiding overplaying a marginal hand.

Q5o is a very weak hand, especially to play out of position in a multi-way pot against a pre-flop raiser and a caller. Despite the decent pot odds, the reverse implied odds and poor playability post-flop make folding the optimal choice.

I am out of position in the small blind with King-high and no pair or significant draw on a Jack-high board. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play here as I have very little equity to bet for value or protection. I will look for a cheap showdown if possible, but prepare to fold to any significant aggression from Flash.

I raised pre-flop with 8h 7s from the button and Flashlight called from the small blind. The flop comes Jh 4d 2h, which is a relatively dry board that favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Flashlight has checked to me, which is often a sign of weakness. I will place a standard continuation bet of 350 (half the pot) to take advantage of my position and initiative, aiming to force a fold from hands that missed the flop.

I am facing a continuation bet on a dry board that I have completely missed with King-high. While the pot odds are tempting, I am out of position and have no pair, flush draw, or straight draw. Calling here would be a 'hope-and-pray' play with no future equity. Given the stack depths and the tournament context, preserving chips to find a better spot where I have at least some realization of equity is the disciplined and professional choice.

I am on the button with Ac7c, which is a solid hand to play in position. With the pot at 150 and blinds at 50/100, raising to 300 is a standard opening move to take the initiative and potentially steal the blinds or build a pot where I have positional advantage.
6d 8h is a weak offsuit one-gapper. Facing a raise from the Button, playing this hand out of position in the Small Blind is highly unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Seven of hearts, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight has opened from the button for 300. While calling is an option to see a flop, 3-betting allows me to take control of the pot and capitalize on my range advantage. Given Flashlight has the shortest stack, a 3-bet puts significant pressure on their opening range. I will raise to 1000 to either take the pot down now or build a pot with a strong suited Ace.

I opened on the button and Flash 3-bet from the big blind. With A7 suited, I have a playable hand that retains value against a 3-bet. While I am not thrilled to be in a bloated pot without a premium holding, folding here would be too weak given my positional advantage. I will call the additional 700 to see the flop and re-evaluate.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-Seven suited to take control of the hand, but this flop of Jack-Nine-Eight is highly coordinated and favors the caller's range more than mine. While I have an overcard and a gutshot straight draw to a Ten, betting into a board that hits many of the middling pairs and connectors Flashlight would call a 3-bet with (like Queen-Jack, Jack-Ten, or even pocket pairs) is risky. I will check to see how the opponent reacts, opting for pot control and hoping to see a free turn card to improve my hand or realize my equity.

I am on the button with Ac7c on a 9d 8c Jh board in a 3-bet pot. Flash has checked to me. My hand has very little equity at the moment, only backdoor flush potential and an Ace-high that is unlikely to be the best hand. Betting here as a bluff into a range that called a 3-bet on a board that hits the big blind's range well (many 9s, 8s, and broadways) is risky. Taking a free card by checking back is the most prudent play to potentially improve or minimize losses.

The turn King of hearts is an excellent card for my range as the pre-flop 3-bettor, and it significantly improves my hand's equity. I now have the nut flush draw to go along with my gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). Since Flashlight checked back on a very coordinated flop (J-9-8), they likely have a marginal made hand or a weak draw that they are trying to take to showdown cheaply. By betting now, I apply pressure to hands like mid-pairs (Tens, Nines) or weak Jacks, while also building a pot for the times I hit my massive draw on the river. A bet of 1000 (roughly half the pot) is large enough to fold out their air and weak pairs while priced reasonably for my semi-bluff.

I am in a 3-bet pot on a board of 9d-8c-Jh-Kh. Flash 3-bet pre-flop, and on the turn, they led out for 1000 into a pot of 2050. My hand, Ace-high, is extremely weak here. Flash's 3-bet range interacts very well with this board (containing many broadways, 9s, 8s, and pocket pairs). Given that I have no straight draws, no flush draws, and very little showdown value, calling 1000 to win a pot that is likely already heavily weighted towards Flash's range is mathematically and strategically incorrect. Folding is the standard professional play here.
I am dealt 2c5d on the Button, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With two players behind me to act, it is a straightforward fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded. This leaves me and Flashlight (Big Blind). Ten-Trey is a very weak hand, even in a blind-versus-blind situation. While it only costs 50 more to call, the hand has poor playability post-flop and I would be out of position. Given my healthy stack and the late stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold junk hands rather than invest chips in marginal spots.

I am on the button with Jc 7s. This is a speculative hand, but I have a large stack (11,200) and the button gives me positional advantage throughout the hand. A standard 3x raise (300) is effective here to steal the blinds or take control of the pot early. Pro in the Big Blind and Flashlight in the Small Blind have been reasonably active, but I want to apply pressure from late position.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h-Ts, a weak offsuit hand. While the pot odds are tempting, playing this hand out of position against an aggressive button raiser like Flash is suboptimal. Given my stack size and the late stage of the tournament, I should prioritize maintaining my remaining chips and wait for a more favorable spot to enter the pot.
Q2o is a very weak hand, far below the threshold for calling or 3-betting against a Button open. Even with the pot odds in the Big Blind, folding is the standard and correct play.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand. Being on the button, I want to take the initiative, build value, and potentially thin the field. A raise to 300 is a standard opening size that I have frequently used in this tournament to assert control.
Facing a raise from the Button, T8o from the Small Blind is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. I'll simply fold to conserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. Flashlight, the shortest stack at the table, has raised to 300 from the Button. Pro has folded from the Small Blind. I need to call only 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 650. Ace-Five is a strong enough hand to defend against a Button open, especially given the pot odds. While 3-betting is an option to put pressure on the short stack, Flashlight's stack is small enough that a call allows me to play post-flop with a positional disadvantage but with a hand that has good high-card value and potential. I will call to see the flop.

I have top pair with an Ace on an extremely coordinated monotone board (Ac Kc 9c). While I have the best pair, my kicker (5) is weak, and the presence of three clubs makes the board very dangerous. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, Flashlight, the best course of action is to check to control the size of the pot and see how they react to this board texture. This allows me to gauge the strength of their hand without committing more chips immediately.

I have flopped top two pair with Ace-King on a relatively wet board (9c, Kc, Ac) that features a potential flush draw. With the opponent checking to me, betting is the optimal move to gain value from weaker kings, lower aces, or middle pairs, while also providing protection by charging any potential flush draws or straight draws to see the next card. A bet of 325, which is exactly half of the current 650 pot, is a standard and effective sizing for this situation.

I have top pair (Aces) with a weak kicker and no club on a highly coordinated monotone board. While the board is dangerous because any club significantly increases my opponent's equity or completes a flush, folding top pair to a single half-pot bet is too conservative given the pot odds (3:1). Flashlight's bet could represent a variety of hands, including a flush, a draw with a high club, a weaker pair, or even a pure continuation bet. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me or have huge equity against me. Therefore, calling is the most prudent action to control the pot size while seeing how the turn develops.

I have top pair with an Ace on a very coordinated board featuring three clubs and several straight possibilities. Since I do not hold a club, I am in a vulnerable position if my opponent has a flush or a high club draw. Checking is the most strategic move here to control the size of the pot and gather more information based on Flashlight's action. I am looking to reach a showdown as cheaply as possible or potentially catch a bluff if they bet again, but I do not want to build a larger pot out of position against a range that could easily contain flushes or straights.

The board has become significantly more coordinated with the Ten of Spades on the turn, which completes potential straight combinations like Queen-Jack. Additionally, the board features three clubs, meaning a flush is possible. While my top two pair (Ace-King) is strong, it is now vulnerable to these made hands. Betting at this point could result in being called only by hands that have me beat or being check-raised by a monster. Checking back is the most prudent play for pot control, allowing me to reach the river and re-evaluate without further inflating the pot against a range that likely connected with this turn card.

On this board (Ac Kc 9c Ts Ks), my hand of Ah 5s represents two pair, Aces and Kings, with a Ten kicker (Ah Ac Ks Kc Ts). This is a very vulnerable hand given the board texture. There are three clubs on the board, making a flush possible. There is also a straight possibility with QJ. Furthermore, with two Kings on the board, any player holding a King has trips. Flashlight checked the turn, which could indicate a medium-strength hand or a trap. Since I am out of position and my hand is likely either losing to a variety of better holdings (flushes, straights, or trips) or chopping with other weak Aces, checking is the most prudent action to reach a showdown as cheaply as possible.

My previous assessment was too conservative and failed to recognize the strength of my hand. The river King (Ks) has given me a full house (Kings full of Aces), which is an extremely strong hand. Given that I am in position and the opponent has checked, this is a clear value-betting opportunity to extract chips from any flushes, straights, or smaller Aces that the opponent might have in their range. Betting 700 into the 1300 pot is a solid, standard value bet.

Analyzing the board (Ac Kc 9c Ts Ks) and my hand (Ah 5s), my best possible hand is two pair, Aces and Kings, with a Ten kicker (Ah-Ac-Ks-Kc-Ts). On this specific board, this hand is extremely weak for several reasons. First, I lose to any King (trips), any flush (three clubs are on the board), and any straight (Q-J). Second, even if my opponent only has an Ace, I only chop the pot with A2 through A9; I lose to AJ and AQ because their kicker (Jack or Queen) would replace the board's Ten. Flashlight's button-raising range is wide and contains many Kings, AJ, AQ, QJ, and clubs. Although Flashlight checked the turn, the river King pairing the board significantly changes the hand, and his bet of 700 into 1300 suggests he is either value-betting a hand that beats mine or hoping to push me off a chop. Given that I lose to a large portion of his range and only chop with the bottom of it, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.
99 is a premium pair, especially 3-handed. Opening to 3x the big blind (300) from the Button is standard to build the pot and thin the field.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c4c, a speculative suited hand. Pro opened to 3 times the big blind from the button. While I have playability and implied odds, 64 suited is quite weak against a button opening range that has been fairly aggressive. My stack is over 100 big blinds, but calling out of position against Pro usually leads to difficult spots post-flop, and 3-betting such a weak hand as a bluff is risky given the current tournament stage (Hand 130/150). I will choose the conservative route and fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh-2h. The button (Pro) has raised to 300. I have 100 already in the pot, so it is 200 to call. Getting 2.25:1 odds on a suited King is a standard call in position-neutral terms, even if the hand is weak. I will call the raise and see a flop.

I have flopped top two pair (Kings and Deuces) on a 2s-7s-Kd board. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot against the button raiser. I am the Big Blind, so I act first. Checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard play here to allow him to continue with his continuation betting range, which I currently dominate. I plan to call or check-raise if he bets.

With pocket nines on a K-7-2 flop, I have a marginal made hand (second pair). Checking back allows me to control the pot size, avoid getting check-raised by stronger hands or strong draws, and keeps the opponent's range wide, including their missed overcards and bluffs, which I can evaluate on the turn.

I have two pair (Kings and Sevens) with a deuce kicker on a board of 2s-7s-Kd-7h. The action has been checked through on both the flop and the turn. Given that I am in the Big Blind and the opponent has checked behind twice, it is highly likely that he is either trapping or has a weak hand that he's given up on. With my two pair, I have a very strong hand. I will value bet on the river to extract chips from any pairs or missed draws he might have. A bet of 500 into a 650 pot is appropriate to get value without overcommitting if he is slow-playing a monster like a seven.

My pocket nines are still a strong bluff-catcher. By checking the flop, I underrepresented my hand, which could induce Flashlight to bluff with missed overcards, flush draws, or smaller pocket pairs. The paired 7 on the turn makes it less likely he holds a 7, leaving his strong value range mostly to Kx. Calling the turn bet allows me to catch bluffs and re-evaluate on the river.

I have two pair, Kings and Sevens, with a 3-kicker (since there is a 3 on the board). The board is 2-7-K-7-3, meaning any King in the opponent's range with a kicker higher than 3 (which is almost all of them) beats my hand. Furthermore, any Seven gives the opponent trips. Given that the opponent called my turn lead, it is highly likely they have a King or a Seven. Betting here would only be called by a better hand, while checking allows me to potentially see a showdown cheaply or catch a bluff. Checking is the most prudent action.

Flashlight's river check indicates he likely missed a draw or holds a marginal showdown hand like a smaller pocket pair or A-high. While my pocket nines have solid showdown value, a value bet here is very thin; a King would likely check-call, and worse hands will almost certainly fold. Checking behind guarantees we get to showdown without risking more chips against a trap or a stubbornly played top pair.

I am on the button with a speculative suited hand (7d 4d). In a three-handed game, opening from the button with suited connectors or gappers is standard to put pressure on the blinds. My standard raise size throughout the tournament has been 300, and I will maintain that consistency here to attempt to take down the blinds or play the hand with positional advantage.

My holding of 4s9h is a very weak starting hand with little potential. Given my position in the small blind and the raise from the button, I do not have the pot odds or the hand strength to justify a call. Folding is the most disciplined and correct long-term strategy to preserve my stack for a better spot.

With A5o in the Big Blind facing a 3bb raise from the Button, we are out of position with a hand that struggles to realize its equity post-flop. While it has some blocker value, A5 offsuit does not play well enough to justify calling a 3x raise, and we lack the playability of a suited Ace to use as a 3-bet bluff or call.
